- Why Marriott, Hilton and Hyatt Don’t Actually Own Most of Their Hotels  | WSJ The Economics Of: Kind: captions Language: en - [Narrator] There's something going on in the hotel industry. Just look at this one block in Chicago. These might seem like three different hotels, but they're actually all Marriott. Over the past 20 years, Marriott has more than tripled in size. And we can trace this boom back to a decision Marriott made decades ago, to move away from the real estate business and instead, commodify its name. - Well, of course, as you might imagine, we think we're the best. We are the largest, 8,700 hotels in 139 countries and territories, really showing expertise around the world for what we think hospitality means. - [Narrator] And you can see the same happening for competitors like Hilton, and now Hyatt, because they're also following a similar playbook. - It's commonplace to actually invest behind real estate in order to build a brand, and then over time, sell down real estate. We wanted to do it in a very deliberate way. - [Narrator] With this business model, the brands don't have to run most of the hotels that fly their flags, nor do they have to pay for them. In most cases, that responsibility falls on people like this guy. - So independent owner-operators like us generally own the hotels and employ the team members. Most people think that they are Marriott employees. They're generally MCR employees. - [Narrator] So why did this shift happen, and how does it affect the customers? This is the economics of hotel chains. The business today looks like this. These are the names you're familiar with, Marriott, Hilton, Hyatt. And these are the players who actually front the money to buy the real estate, hire the employees, and run the day-to-day operations. Owners pay to use these big hotel names, referred to as flags in the industry, and it's a departure from the tried and true strategy of decades before. - [Tyler] If you go back to the 50s, everything was owned, operated, and flagged by the same person. - [Narrator] MCR is the third largest hotel owner-operator behind these two real estate investment trusts. - We get all the revenue, and then we get whatever profits are left over. And we pay Marriott or Hilton a franchise fee to be part of their system. - [Narrator] That means hotel brands mainly have money to gain with every new hotel. - Because they were not on the hook for the asset cost, they were able to scale up their brands much faster. - [Narrator] Moving to franchising removed a lot of the financial risks associated with real estate. - One of the things we realized as those hotels got more and more popular is that by building them all on balance sheet and with dealing with economic cycles, we were constrained. Our growth was constrained. - [Narrator] Now, Marriott and Hilton each own less than 1% of their properties, while Hyatt owns about 2%. But in shifting to this strategy, major hotel brands now have to focus on a new customer. - The owner is the one that puts the brand onto their hotel, and that's what drives franchise fees to the brands. - [Narrator] Those fees can amount to anywhere from 5 to 15% of what the property brings in. So the brands have to prove that having the name is worth it. Hotel brands say. - It more than pays for itself. - [Narrator] Because in addition to data on how lobby should look and what amenities to have, they also provide data to help the owner get the most money for each room each night. - At the end of the day, you are saying, "What will the market bear?" When there's a Taylor Swift concert coming into a market, we see the rates literally out to 40 miles away go crazy. - [Narrator] In dynamic cities, the price of a room could change multiple times in one day. - We get questions all the time and they say, "I was gonna book for $199, and now the rate just went up to $249." You know, then you should have booked at the 199. The prices are fleeting. - Every one of our full-service hotels has a revenue manager, and they take account of what's going on in that particular marketplace. - [Narrator] And these systems also calculate rates that optimize for the hotel's bottom line. - [Tyler] We don't wanna sell out our hotels just on a Tuesday because then that leaves us exposed on Wednesday. So we may sell you that room at an extraordinarily high price because what we really want is for you to book Monday to Wednesday or Tuesday to Thursday. - [Narrator] This helps make up for less profitable days. - We're giving the rooms away on Sundays. It is the worst night of the week because business travel does not really occur on Sundays and neither does leisure travel. - [Narrator] Flying a flag can also help owners target wider pools of hotel customers, with better fees for booking platforms like Expedia and Booking.com. - So if you buy a hotel room via Expedia for a Marriott hotel, we pay a lower Expedia fee. We, the owner. - [Narrator] But analysts say an even bigger draw, "Our hotel loyalty programs." Marriott and Hilton each have over 180 million members. Hyatt has more than 40 million. And thanks to points, guests are incentivized to shop within these programs. - The points have essentially become currency. - [Narrator] Which can help increase the customer base for flagged hotels in smaller markets. - If you're in a secondary or tertiary market, you generally fly a flag because that brings people to your building 'cause they want the points. The idea is you earn the points in Corpus Christi or in Yuma, Arizona, and then you redeem them in Miami and New York. - [Narrator] Today, two-thirds of all hotels in the US are branded, but the franchise model isn't always beneficial. For owners in high-demand markets, like those with more leisure travel, independence might be a better option. - The highest performing hotels in Manhattan are independent hotels. You have a greater elasticity of demand. So usually, the highest performing hotels are smaller. - [Narrator] But brands do operate some properties mainly in this tier, whereas franchising mostly happens in these. - Those operations have dramatically fewer touch points and offerings by virtue of the fact that they're designed for a person who wants a great room to stay in, a quick option for breakfast, but they don't need other things. They don't need a ballroom. They don't need a specialty restaurant. They don't need a spa. - [Narrator] In the luxury tier, those amenities are expected, creating more complex logistics. That's why most luxury hotels are still operated by the hotel brand itself. - With respect to most other full service and luxury properties and resorts, we would prefer to manage because we wanna control all aspects of the delivery of the guest experience. - Specifically at the luxury end, we do overwhelmingly manage those hotels, and we wouldn't, for example, be talking about franchising an EDITION Hotel. What having this range of brands gives us the ability to appeal to our customer for every experience and location that they want, and that is really important for Bonvoy. - [Narrator] In some rare cases, hotel brands will still strategically purchase a property. Marriott bought this W Hotel in New York to use it as an incubator for new ideas and designs, and Hyatt purchased Hotel Irvine. - We bought the hotel. We completely renovated it. We will eventually sell that hotel. - [Narrator] But by and large, more hotels are outsourcing the owning and operating increasing the volume of branded properties offered to consumers today. - The customer now has more choices. They have more places to both earn and redeem. But what you have seen in the past 25 years is a consolidation. There are really three or four big hotel flag families now. - [Narrator] And looking at the properties in construction, the number of branded hotels is only set to increase.

It’s one&nbsp; of those pivot years where guest expectations,&nbsp;&nbsp; technology, and the way people travel all shift&nbsp; at the same time. The operators who see the&nbsp;&nbsp; shift early and adapt are going to feel a lot more&nbsp; confident and in control over the next few years,&nbsp;&nbsp; and the ones who don’t are going to wonder why&nbsp; the same playbook suddenly stopped working. In&nbsp;&nbsp; this video, I’m going to walk you through the top&nbsp; five hospitality trends for 2026 that I’m seeing&nbsp;&nbsp; across hotels, villas, and short-term rentals&nbsp; worldwide, in a way that’s practical and grounded&nbsp;&nbsp; in day-to-day operations, not theory. And if you&nbsp; stay with me all the way through, I’ll give you&nbsp;&nbsp; a simple way at the end to decide which one or&nbsp; two of these trends you should act on first in&nbsp;&nbsp; your own business, so you finish this video with&nbsp; a clear next step, not just more information.&nbsp; Before we dive in, I need to be very clear about&nbsp; what this channel is and what it isn’t. This&nbsp;&nbsp; video is for educational purposes only. I do not&nbsp; offer financial advice or investment services.&nbsp;&nbsp; I’m not asking you to invest in anything, I’m&nbsp; not making promises about returns, and nothing&nbsp;&nbsp; here should be taken as a recommendation to buy,&nbsp; sell, or hold any type of asset. Any references I&nbsp;&nbsp; make to blockchain, tokenization, or DeFi are&nbsp; purely informational and always tied back to&nbsp;&nbsp; how hospitality and real estate operations are&nbsp; evolving, not how anyone should manage their&nbsp;&nbsp; personal finances. If you ever need financial&nbsp; or legal advice, please speak with a qualified&nbsp;&nbsp; professional who knows your specific situation.&nbsp; My goal here is simple: to share what I’m seeing&nbsp;&nbsp; in the hospitality world after more than&nbsp; four decades in this industry so you can&nbsp;&nbsp; make better decisions in your own business. I’ve spent over forty years in hospitality&nbsp;&nbsp; – owning and operating hotels, working with&nbsp; brands like Marriott, Hilton, and Holiday Inn,&nbsp;&nbsp; and more recently focusing on luxury short-term&nbsp; rentals and villas in international markets. I’ve&nbsp;&nbsp; seen this industry through recessions, booms, new&nbsp; distribution channels, and a whole lot of change,&nbsp;&nbsp; but what’s happening right now feels different&nbsp; because the changes are happening all at once.&nbsp;&nbsp; Guests are more digital, more mobile, more&nbsp; value-conscious, and at the same time more&nbsp;&nbsp; demanding about experience, sustainability,&nbsp; and wellness. Technology has finally caught&nbsp;&nbsp; up to a lot of the ideas we used to talk about&nbsp; as “the future,” and now it’s on our doorstep&nbsp;&nbsp; as a practical tool, not a futuristic concept. Let’s start with the first big trend for 2026,&nbsp;&nbsp; which is the shift from technology being an add-on&nbsp; to becoming a core part of how hospitality runs&nbsp;&nbsp; day to day. A few years ago, AI chatbots,&nbsp; digital check-in, and smart room controls&nbsp;&nbsp; sounded like nice extras that only big brands&nbsp; or cutting-edge properties could justify. Today,&nbsp;&nbsp; we’re at a point where guests actually expect some&nbsp; level of automation and self-service, especially&nbsp;&nbsp; for simple things. They’re used to messaging apps&nbsp; and instant answers. They don’t want to stand in&nbsp;&nbsp; line to ask what time breakfast starts or to&nbsp; request extra towels. At the same time, your&nbsp;&nbsp; team is under pressure from staffing shortages&nbsp; and rising labor costs. That creates a natural&nbsp;&nbsp; push toward AI-powered tools: automated messaging,&nbsp; task assignment, basic pre-arrival communication,&nbsp;&nbsp; and even predictive maintenance in the background.&nbsp; When this is done right, technology isn’t&nbsp;&nbsp; replacing people; it’s taking friction out of the&nbsp; simple interactions so your staff can focus on&nbsp;&nbsp; the moments that actually require a human touch –&nbsp; resolving problems, creating surprise-and-delight&nbsp;&nbsp; experiences, and building real loyalty. If you run a small hotel or a handful of villas,&nbsp;&nbsp; you might be thinking, “That’s great for the big&nbsp; guys, but I don’t have a tech department.” The&nbsp;&nbsp; good news is you don’t need one. Many of the&nbsp; tools coming into the market are cloud-based,&nbsp;&nbsp; subscription-style, and designed for&nbsp; operators who are not tech experts.&nbsp;&nbsp; The key, especially in 2026, is to pick one&nbsp; or two automation points that will make the&nbsp;&nbsp; biggest difference, instead of trying to&nbsp; overhaul everything at once. For example,&nbsp;&nbsp; maybe your first step is adding a branded&nbsp; guest messaging system that integrates with&nbsp;&nbsp; your property management system so that every&nbsp; question, from directions to late check-out,&nbsp;&nbsp; flows into one place. Or maybe you start&nbsp; with digital check-in and smart locks in&nbsp;&nbsp; your villas, so you’re not coordinating key&nbsp; hand-offs at odd hours. Over time, you can&nbsp;&nbsp; layer on more: housekeeping automation, simple&nbsp; AI assistants that answer routine questions,&nbsp;&nbsp; even basic analytics that show you which questions&nbsp; guests ask most often so you can fix root causes.&nbsp; The second trend is what I’d call real&nbsp; personalization, not just putting someone’s&nbsp;&nbsp; name in the confirmation email. Guests in 2026&nbsp; are absolutely flooded with options – hundreds of&nbsp;&nbsp; hotels in a city, thousands of short-term rentals&nbsp; in a region – and they’re starting to gravitate&nbsp;&nbsp; toward stays that feel like they were designed for&nbsp; them. Personalization shows up in little details:&nbsp;&nbsp; knowing whether a guest prefers a firm pillow or&nbsp; a soft one, offering a quiet room to a business&nbsp;&nbsp; traveler by default, having non-alcoholic&nbsp; options ready for someone who doesn’t drink,&nbsp;&nbsp; or making sure a family with young kids doesn’t&nbsp; end up in a room right above the bar. A lot of&nbsp;&nbsp; this doesn’t require a huge budget; it requires&nbsp; better use of the information you already have&nbsp;&nbsp; and a mindset shift from “every guest is&nbsp; the same” to “every guest has a profile.”&nbsp; Technology helps here too. Modern hospitality&nbsp; systems and even simple CRM tools give you the&nbsp;&nbsp; ability to capture preferences, tag guests, and&nbsp; keep notes that actually get used on the next&nbsp;&nbsp; stay. In 2026, the properties that stand&nbsp; out are the ones that connect those dots.&nbsp;&nbsp; That could look like a pre-arrival message&nbsp; asking, “Are you traveling for work, leisure,&nbsp;&nbsp; or both?” or “Are you bringing kids or pets?” and&nbsp; then using the answers to shape the stay – from&nbsp;&nbsp; room assignment to local recommendations. For&nbsp; a luxury villa, it could mean pre-stocking the&nbsp;&nbsp; fridge based on a short questionnaire, arranging&nbsp; a private chef for the first night, or making sure&nbsp;&nbsp; the workspace is set up properly for a guest who&nbsp; plans to work remote while they’re there. When&nbsp;&nbsp; a guest feels seen and taken care of, not just&nbsp; processed, they’re far more likely to come back,&nbsp;&nbsp; leave positive reviews, and tell their friends. Trend number three is the continued rise and&nbsp;&nbsp; professionalization of luxury short-term rentals&nbsp; and villas. For a long time, hotels and short-term&nbsp;&nbsp; rentals were treated like two separate&nbsp; universes. Hotels were the professional&nbsp;&nbsp; choice with standards and brand consistency,&nbsp; and short-term rentals were the more casual,&nbsp;&nbsp; home-like option that could be hit or miss. That&nbsp; line has blurred dramatically. We now see more&nbsp;&nbsp; professionally managed villas and short-term&nbsp; rentals that deliver hotel-level service:&nbsp;&nbsp; airport transfers, concierge, housekeeping, chef&nbsp; services, and curated experiences, especially in&nbsp;&nbsp; the luxury segment. At the same time, many hotels&nbsp; have started to look more like short-term rentals,&nbsp;&nbsp; offering apartment-style rooms, kitchenettes,&nbsp; flexible layouts, and co-living options.&nbsp; In 2026, travelers at the higher end of the&nbsp; market are often looking for three things at once:&nbsp;&nbsp; privacy, space, and reliability. A well-run villa&nbsp; or short-term rental can check all three boxes if&nbsp;&nbsp; it’s operated with hotel-grade standards. Guests&nbsp; want the feeling of having their own home – their&nbsp;&nbsp; own pool, their own living spaces – but they&nbsp; also want to know the linens will be spotless,&nbsp;&nbsp; the wifi will work, and someone will pick up the&nbsp; phone at eleven at night if there’s a problem.&nbsp;&nbsp; If you’re in the villa or short-term&nbsp; rental space, that’s your opportunity:&nbsp;&nbsp; treating each property like a micro-hotel with&nbsp; clear standards, documented procedures, and a&nbsp;&nbsp; consistent guest journey. If you’re in hotels,&nbsp; this trend is a reminder that your competition&nbsp;&nbsp; may not look like another branded hotel down&nbsp; the street; it may be a single incredible&nbsp;&nbsp; villa on the coast, or a cluster of high-end&nbsp; apartments run by a professional operator.&nbsp; There’s a regulatory side to this as well. Around&nbsp; the world, we’re seeing tighter rules in some&nbsp;&nbsp; urban markets and more interest in expanding&nbsp; tourism to secondary and rural locations.&nbsp;&nbsp; That doesn’t mean the short-term rental story&nbsp; is over; it means we’re moving toward a more&nbsp;&nbsp; mature stage where professional operations,&nbsp; compliance, and community impact all matter.&nbsp;&nbsp; As an operator, you want to be on the right side&nbsp; of that evolution: transparent with local rules,&nbsp;&nbsp; respectful of neighbors, and thoughtful about how&nbsp; your properties fit into the broader destination.&nbsp;&nbsp; Long term, that’s what will separate sustainable&nbsp; operations from the ones that get squeezed out.&nbsp; The fourth big trend for 2026 is that&nbsp; sustainability and wellness have moved&nbsp;&nbsp; from marketing buzzwords to baseline expectations.&nbsp; Guests increasingly care about how you operate,&nbsp;&nbsp; not just what your property looks like.&nbsp; They’re asking questions about energy use,&nbsp;&nbsp; waste, water, and local impact, even if they&nbsp; never say it out loud. At the same time,&nbsp;&nbsp; many travelers are looking for stays that help&nbsp; them feel better – physically and mentally – not&nbsp;&nbsp; just entertained. Wellness tourism isn’t just&nbsp; yoga retreats anymore; it’s everything from&nbsp;&nbsp; good sleep environments and quiet spaces to&nbsp; healthy food options and access to nature.&nbsp; For hotels, this can start with simple, measurable&nbsp; steps: LED lighting, smart thermostats, better&nbsp;&nbsp; insulation, water-saving fixtures, and thoughtful&nbsp; housekeeping practices that reduce waste without&nbsp;&nbsp; compromising cleanliness. It also means being&nbsp; honest – if you’re not a fully eco property, don’t&nbsp;&nbsp; pretend to be, but show guests what you are doing&nbsp; and where you’re headed. For villas and short-term&nbsp;&nbsp; rentals, sustainability might mean partnering with&nbsp; local suppliers instead of importing everything,&nbsp;&nbsp; installing solar where it makes sense, offering&nbsp; filtered water instead of endless plastic bottles,&nbsp;&nbsp; and designing outdoor spaces that work with&nbsp; the local environment instead of against it.&nbsp; On the wellness side, think about what a guest’s&nbsp; body and mind actually go through when they&nbsp;&nbsp; travel: jet lag, inconsistent sleep, rich food,&nbsp; screen fatigue, and constant stimulation. You&nbsp;&nbsp; don’t have to build a full spa to respond. You&nbsp; can start with blackout curtains, comfortable&nbsp;&nbsp; mattresses, quiet air conditioning, spaces with&nbsp; natural light, maybe a small corner with yoga&nbsp;&nbsp; mats or a simple stretch guide. For villas, that&nbsp; could include outdoor showers, shaded work areas,&nbsp;&nbsp; or partnerships with local massage therapists,&nbsp; trainers, or guides who can come to the property.&nbsp;&nbsp; The goal is to help people leave feeling better&nbsp; than when they arrived, not more exhausted.&nbsp; Trend number five is the deepening of bleisure&nbsp; and work-from-anywhere travel. For years,&nbsp;&nbsp; people talked about combining business and&nbsp; leisure travel; now it’s baked into how a&nbsp;&nbsp; lot of people live and work. Remote and hybrid&nbsp; work models mean guests are booking longer stays,&nbsp;&nbsp; working during the day and exploring mornings,&nbsp; evenings, or weekends. For hospitality operators,&nbsp;&nbsp; that changes what a good stay looks like. It’s not&nbsp; just a nice bed and a good shower; it’s a reliable&nbsp;&nbsp; workspace, serious Wi-Fi, plenty of outlets, and&nbsp; spaces where someone can take a video call without&nbsp;&nbsp; feeling like they’re in the middle of a bar. In 2026, many destinations are positioning&nbsp;&nbsp; themselves specifically as hubs for digital nomads&nbsp; or remote workers. For hotels, that might mean&nbsp;&nbsp; transforming certain areas into co-working-style&nbsp; lounges, offering weekly or monthly rates with&nbsp;&nbsp; extras like laundry and simple printing, and&nbsp; adjusting your food and beverage to support people&nbsp;&nbsp; who are on property all day instead of away at&nbsp; the office. For villas and short-term rentals, it&nbsp;&nbsp; might mean designing at least one room or corner&nbsp; as a proper workspace, making sure the chairs and&nbsp;&nbsp; desk heights are comfortable, clearly stating your&nbsp; internet speeds, and offering stay lengths that&nbsp;&nbsp; make sense for remote workers – not just two-night&nbsp; weekends, but ten-night or one-month packages.&nbsp; Longer stays also affect how you think about&nbsp; housekeeping, pricing, and community. Guests&nbsp;&nbsp; staying for two or three weeks often want&nbsp; a different level of integration with the&nbsp;&nbsp; local area. They might be more interested in&nbsp; grocery stores, gyms, coworking spaces, and&nbsp;&nbsp; schools than museums and tours. If you understand&nbsp; that and build it into your communication – your&nbsp;&nbsp; pre-arrival emails, your digital guidebooks,&nbsp; or your front-desk conversations – you can turn&nbsp;&nbsp; your property into a base for people’s&nbsp; real lives, not just a break from them.&nbsp; Underneath all five of these trends&nbsp; – automation, personalization,&nbsp;&nbsp; professional villas, sustainability and wellness,&nbsp; and work-from-anywhere travel – there’s a common&nbsp;&nbsp; thread: the industry is becoming more data-driven&nbsp; and more connected. That’s where some of the&nbsp;&nbsp; newer technology like blockchain and tokenization&nbsp; quietly sits in the background. You don’t have to&nbsp;&nbsp; be a crypto person to benefit from better digital&nbsp; infrastructure. Over time, we’re going to see more&nbsp;&nbsp; secure ways to manage guest identity, loyalty,&nbsp; and bookings; more transparent ways to see where&nbsp;&nbsp; your money is going in the operational stack;&nbsp; and maybe even new forms of digital credits or&nbsp;&nbsp; loyalty points that guests can earn and use across&nbsp; different types of stays. When I talk about those&nbsp;&nbsp; tools, it’s always in the context of operations&nbsp; – how they might simplify reconciliations,&nbsp;&nbsp; reduce fraud, or make it easier to reward your&nbsp; best guests – not as something to speculate on.&nbsp; So, what do you actually do with all of this if&nbsp; you’re watching and feeling a little overwhelmed?&nbsp;&nbsp; The first step is to be honest about where&nbsp; you are. You don’t have to implement every&nbsp;&nbsp; trend tomorrow. Maybe your biggest pain&nbsp; right now is staff time at the front desk;&nbsp;&nbsp; in that case, starting with a good guest&nbsp; messaging platform and clear pre-arrival&nbsp;&nbsp; communication could change your life. Maybe your&nbsp; reviews mention that guests loved your staff but&nbsp;&nbsp; wished the property felt more up to date; that&nbsp; might be your signal to focus on small touches&nbsp;&nbsp; of personalization and sustainability that&nbsp; guests can see and feel. If your occupancy&nbsp;&nbsp; is solid on weekends but soft midweek, maybe you&nbsp; experiment with remote-worker friendly packages&nbsp;&nbsp; that include faster Wi-Fi, workspaces, and&nbsp; a slightly different housekeeping cadence.&nbsp; Here’s the simple filter I promised you at the&nbsp; beginning. As you think about these five trends,&nbsp;&nbsp; ask yourself three quick questions. First, which&nbsp; trend is already showing up in my reviews or guest&nbsp;&nbsp; comments, even indirectly? Second, which trend,&nbsp; if I made a small move in the next thirty days,&nbsp;&nbsp; would my guests actually notice and appreciate?&nbsp; And third, which trend would genuinely make&nbsp;&nbsp; life easier for my team, not just add another&nbsp; task? The trend that checks all three boxes is&nbsp;&nbsp; usually the one you should start with. You&nbsp; don’t need a fifteen-page strategy plan;&nbsp;&nbsp; you need one or two concrete changes,&nbsp; tested over the next quarter, that move&nbsp;&nbsp; you closer to where the industry is clearly going. Let me also repeat the disclaimer before we wrap&nbsp;&nbsp; up because it matters. Everything I’m sharing&nbsp; here is based on my experience in hospitality&nbsp;&nbsp; and on what I’m seeing across the industry right&nbsp; now. This video is for educational purposes only.&nbsp;&nbsp; I do not offer financial advice or investment&nbsp; services. Any mention of blockchain, tokenization,&nbsp;&nbsp; or other technologies is purely informational&nbsp; and strictly related to how hospitality and real&nbsp;&nbsp; estate operations are evolving. If you&nbsp; have financial, tax, or legal questions,&nbsp;&nbsp; please speak with a licensed professional&nbsp; who understands your specific situation.&nbsp; If this breakdown of the top hospitality trends&nbsp; for 2026 helped you see your business a little&nbsp;&nbsp; more clearly, feel free to stay on the channel&nbsp; and check out some of the other educational videos&nbsp;&nbsp; here. I’ll keep sharing what I’m seeing in hotels,&nbsp; luxury villas, and short-term rentals, and how&nbsp;&nbsp; operators can navigate this next chapter in a way&nbsp; that’s sustainable, guest-focused, and grounded&nbsp;&nbsp; in reality. Thanks for watching, I’m Daniel&nbsp; Vosotas, and I’ll see you in the next video.

You're going to be without your watch for maybe even up to a year's time because every time you service a watch, especially with the Swiss, it takes a few months to actually get your watch back. So, number one, you'll be without your watch for quite some time. Number two, those services cost money and they are expensive. I would imagine a Christopher Ward, if you need to get that service, it's going to cost less than $1,000, especially if the watch cost $1,000. So this is all relative. I'm talking about luxury brands here. So definitely keep that in mind. So here's my proposal. Instead of servicing your watch every 5 years, why not just wear your watch until it stops working? So in other words, it stops telling time or it's not telling time accurately or if there's a function on the watch like a chronograph or a date and those items stop working, you need to get your watch serviced. That's when you bring your watch in and say, "Hey, I need a service." Now, it will not happen within a 5-year interval, especially if that is a brand new watch. If you got your watch serviced 5 years ago, the likelihood of that watch breaking within the next 5 years is very, very low. And it will probably tell very accurate time for many years past that 5-year interval. So you could end up servicing your watch maybe one time in 20 years and the service will cost exactly the same as if you serviced it every single 5-year interval. And for those of you who don't believe that, I'm going to tell you two stories, personal stories of services that I performed that I had done on my watches and the results. So number one, I bought a watch on eBay. It was a Briget Type 20. I bought the watch. I got it. It had a scratch on the sapphire crystal. I complained to the guy that I bought it from and we worked out something that I would be able to go and get a service. It was around $500 off of the price. Otherwise, they would not service the watch whatsoever. That service would cost $1,500. So, off the back of that, I went to another reputable repair shop here in New York City that is not actually associated with Briget or really any brand. I brought it to them and they swapped out the Sapphire Crystal with a Brigade Sapphire Crystal for around $400. It was actually more than I expected it would cost, but that's what they did it for. And I ended up selling that watch later on on eBay anyway with the new crystal on it. But with that aside, the watch was telling perfect time. The chronograph was working perfectly. The only thing that was wrong with it was that there was a scratch on the sapphire crystal and they wanted to do a full overhaul and it would be $1,500. Now, fast forward a few years ago. I was at a flea market here in New York and I saw that they had some vintage watches. I went over and I looked at them and there was an Omega Speed Master Mark 2 in a bin that they were selling for around $600. This was on a bracelet. I actually still have this watch. It is beautiful. I decided to buy it even though the chronograph was not working. It was telling time, but the chronograph was not functioning. and I couldn't live like that. I wanted to have this fixed. Don't replace the dial. you could replace the mineral crystal that it had because it was very scratched up. And that was the only thing that I wanted them to do. Who knows where it actually went, but they did the repair and I got the bill and it was $1,200. $1,200 for a watch that was in very, very rough condition. They also changed the dial and the hands. Gave me the dial and the hands on the side. So, I have the original dial. I have the original hands. They completely overhauled the movement. They took the entire movement apart and they cleaned it and they replaced a bunch of parts in the movement. So, basically, this watch looks brand new when I got it back from Omega from this service, and it was $1,200. Here's the point. If I had brought that in working in really good condition, the overhaul would have cost me $1,200 cuz they would have done the exact same amount of work. That's what they do. When you bring it in for a service, they are only going to do a major service on the watch. That is all they do. If there's a part broken, they are not just going to fix that part. They're going to take apart the entire watch, check for any other issues, put it all back together, and send it back to you as a completely serviced watch. So again, my point here is the 5-year intervals, the 10-year intervals, the 2-year intervals, they mean nothing. Wear your watch until it is not working properly, and then of course you can get it service. Now, there is always an exception to every single rule. And the exception here, I would say, is if you are using your watch as a professional. So, if you're a diver, you have a dive watch that you use to time things and do things underwater, you need to service your watch. You cannot not service your watch here because this is very important. Your life actually depends on it. If you use it for your job, where you're timing things, you use a chronograph, maybe you're in sports, maybe you're in car racing, these things, you need to have a watch that is serviced and working perfectly at its peak. So, I highly recommend keeping those service intervals as suggested. And if your job is important to you or the way that you are timing things is important to you, definitely service your watches. So, those are exceptions to the rule in my opinion. And the other situation where I think servicing really doesn't make sense is if of course you have a large collection. If you have more than one watch, that means you're cutting the amount of time you should have to service your watches in half. If you're wearing your watches equally. So if you're rotating your watches or rotating through your watches on a regular basis, that means your watches are sitting not being used, there's not a lot of stress being put on the movements, that means you don't need to service them incredibly often. And since I'm a watch collector, I have multiple watches. It is rare that I go from one day to the next wearing the exact same watch. That means my watches are sitting most of their life. So that means I don't need to service any watches. I have only serviced maybe three or four watches in my entire watch collecting career. And I've been collecting for over 25 years. I have many, many watches and I barely have serviced any of them. A small, small fraction. So, I'll finish this video up with a little bit of an analogy that I made in that previous video that I spoke about in the past. This is going to be the same analogy. However, I want to iron out some of the finer points. I likened watch collecting to collecting cars, to servicing cars. So, in other words, you have to service your car on a regular interval. However, if it stops working, obviously, you're going to bring it in for service. Servicing cars is different from servicing watches. Even though watch collecting and car collecting have a lot in common, they are very different from a logistical standpoint. You have to service cars on a regular basis. If you don't, you're going to end up getting stranded someplace. You might be driving along and something that you didn't maintain may break and then you will get stranded or worse, you could even get hurt or killed if you're driving at high speeds. If you are not properly maintaining a classic car or even a modern car, it really doesn't matter. Proper maintenance is very, very important. When you're talking about a watch, if you don't maintain your watch on a regular basis, the only thing that's going to happen is you might be late for something. However, we are surrounded by clocks these days. It's not the 80s or 90s. No one comes up to you anymore and asks you, "What time is it?" just because they see that you have a watch on your wrist. Everyone has a phone in their pocket. It's far more accurate than any mechanical watch that you're going to be wearing on your wrist. So, there really is no point to these watches anymore. Everywhere you look, not only on your phone, there are clocks in your car, on your computer. Doesn't matter where you are, there are clocks. And usually those are very accurate timetelling devices because they are atomic or they're getting a radio signal. It doesn't matter. They're more accurate than what's probably on your wrist. If your watch stops working, you're not going to die. You're going to be able to tell the time and then you can just go and get your watch serviced. So, the bottom line, the whole point of this video is if you have a lot of watches or if you have one watch, don't service your watches on regular intervals, save that money up and buy more watches. That's basically the whole point of this video. I service none of my watches. All the money that I would have spent on service, I probably just put that towards more new watches. the integrity of the water resistance of the watch protecting the movement lastly we have full service of the watch which includes a complete disassembly of the movement and the case replacing all the seals the full service of the movement and and therefore returning that watch to as close as we can to the new condition as a watchmaker I'd like to take a few minutes to talk about things you can do as a consumer and the watch owner to both protect your watch maintain your watch and get the best use out of your watch in between service intervals the first thing I can say and anyone that's that's is you pick the watch up off there and you put it on and it's gonna be relatively on time because there's always gonna be some kind of error on the watch it's gonna be running fast it's gonna be running slow you're still gonna have to reset the watch in most cases if you take it off that automatic winder the only the only advantage it gives you is the watch is fully wound but again what I see as a watchmaker is it puts so much more wear and tear on those automatic components keeping that mainspring at full power that it's actually wearing that watch out as it's on that winder you have a condensation on the crystal so long as that condensation goes away dissipates in a minute you're okay the key there is to avoid drastic temperature changes you don't want to go into a hot tub with a watch you don't want to go into a steaming watch I don't like wearing watches in the shower because of the high-pressure water that comes out if there's any leaks any degradation to the crystal or to the to the gaskets of that watch water's gonna find its way in waters death 2 watches maintain your watch maintain your gaskets and you're gonna get a longer lifespan out of your watch it's always safest if you have to change the date again you have your automatic watch you set it down you didn't wear it for a week you have to reset the date the way I do it personally and I'm a watchmaker so you can listen to me or not is you pick that watch up you wind it you go ahead and set the time and rotate it past 12 o'clock let that let you let the calendar change or see that you're at noon if the calendar didn't change then go ahead and use your quick set don't try to use your quick set between 10:00 and 2:00 if you do that you're actually forcing components if if you're you're further along the wear cycle of that watch if you're in the three years or five years into that watch you asking actually risk damaging components inside there Andras that will result in a higher maintenance fee don't use your chronograph in or around water if your watch is wet don't use your chronograph you run the risk of actually pumping water into the watch you put one little drop of water in there over time it's gonna corrode the very delicate and precise components that are inside the watch when you drop your watch the jewels move and they actually spring back into position protecting the balance that's what they're designed to do but you really don't want to drop your watch I'm not saying anyone's gonna do it on purpose but if you drop your watch you know by accident on a hard surface have it checked something could have cracked a crown a tube could have cracked which eventually is gonna allow water into that watch a gasket could have shifted a lot of things could happen the timing could be off on their watch a lot of things could happen from a simple drop I've seen watches dropped on carpets that are completely thrown out of beads and have broken crowns so if you ever dropped your watch have it checked by a watchmaker a common thing we see too is a broken crystal sapphire while scratch-resistant is also very brittle when a sapphire crystal breaks it fractures into very very tiny parts you're gonna take it to your watchmaker your watchmaker is gonna tell you you need a service you're asking why do I need a service I just want the crystal replaced because you potentially have glass inside that movement my recommendation is if you ever break the crystal on your watch immediately stop it pull the crown out if it has a hacking mechanism like Rolex does pull the crown out stop that movement from running that'll also protect your dial you won't be dragging glass around the dial scratching the dial another common concern we have with watches of magnetism more and more watches are being made anti-magnetic by anti-magnetic it doesn't mean the watch's magnetic is less susceptible to magnetism when a watch is magnetized the first indication is it runs extremely fast why does it run fast because you're effectively taking that hair spring that's vibrating and you're shortening that's because it's sticking together that hair spring sticking together it's making the watch run faster it's almost like it's chattering inside there so magnetism is one of the things how do you eat you don't normally get it magnetized by going through an airport you know metal detector that's that's old wives tale it's where you set the watch down the rare earth magnets are very very common in everyday life right now anything that has a clasp a purse an iPad case anything that has a spring clasp on it can magnetize your watch if you set your watch down on it so just be mindful as to where you put your watch where you set it down most times it will watch is magnetized we don't have to open it up we can demagnetize it on the spot and it's fine however again if you drop that watch you run the risk of hooking a hair spring or doing more damage to it when it comes to cleaning the watches you can clean the watch under running water I wouldn't recommend a shower regular running water because it comes out with less pressure than a shower it's not forced through a little hole use a toothbrush use a mild detergent towel dry your watch off I've seen I actually saw a watch one time I saw a Rolex that actually got eaten by a dog passed through the dog and the woman took the watch and put it in her dishwasher and ran it through the dishwasher and we service that watch and they actually came out fine when it comes to maintaining your watch the most important thing is Buildium is also pretty affordable, offering three different plans that increase in cost and features. Most businesses will opt for the growth plan, which starts at $183 per month and increases based on the number of units you manage. So, let's say you manage 100 units, you're looking at around $240 per month. Next up is Appfolio, which works really well if you're juggling both residential and commercial units. Its maintenance management tool pulls everything into one single dashboard. From there, you can see requests from your apartment, residents, and business offices. No more jumping between different screens. At FFolio also lets you create detailed inspection sheets for moveins and move outs. Have an apartment building with different unit styles? No problem. You can provide forms for different room types in the same property, giving you more specific feedback. That's way better than generic templates or even worse, paper checklists. Now, pricing's a bit more opaque with Appfolio, so you need a direct quote for exact rates. That said, there's a 50unit minimum for the lowest pricing package, so if you have less, you'll need to look elsewhere. And the reality is getting exact quotes from a bunch of different vendors is a real hassle. There's a whole process of talking to a sales rep, determining if you're a good fit, and then haggling over price. But good news, there's an easier way. Just visit softwareconnect.com and we'll give you tailored recommendations for free so you can get price quotes and demos without the headache. Now, let's talk Yardy Breeze. This one's really strong on the accounting side. It handles your general ledger, payables, receivables, even escrow and trust accounting without needing a separate tool. It offers packages for a wide range of property types, residential, commercial, affordable housing, even associations and self- storage. The interface is also super clean and makes it easy to find the more niche property details like specific unit types or custom lease terms. Yardy Breeze's pricing depends upon what types of properties you manage. For residential, it starts at $1 per unit per month with a $100 per month minimum. For commercial, it's the same price per unit with a $200 minimum. It's a solid pick for small to midsize operators who want something reliable without breaking the bank. Now, if you're managing condos or HOAs, take a look at Door Loop. With its communication portal, you can blast out communitywide announcements in bulk. Plus, you can assign tasks to staff and share files with the architectural review board to get new projects going fast. Collection fees is simple, too. Door Loop automatically separates your association fees from rent, so everything stays clean and compliant with just a few clicks. You can also set deadlines and track overdue payments. As for pricing, Door Loop's three packages range from $69 to $199 per month when build annually. The price is increased by $20 for every 20 units added, making it a little more cost-effective than a per unit model. That said, the basic plan has a maximum of 160 units. So, if you're above that, you'll need one of the higher tiers. But if you're just getting started, you probably want something at the lowest cost possible. Enter Hemlane, which offers a free plan if you're only managing a couple of properties. You can screen applicants, advertise on websites, and use the full rental accounting module. Granted, there's a lot more features on the paid plan, but when you're just starting to grow, the system scales with more automation and support. So, there you have it. Those are my top property management software picks. And if you want to explore more options, visit our full roundup page in the description. We feature other products like Avail and Tenant Cloud. - Types of vacation rental cleans (and how to manage each one) | Breezeway Academy: Kind: captions Language: en Hi everyone, I'm Karine and welcome back to Breezeway Academy. >> [music] >> At Breezeway Academy, you'll learn how to build stronger, more professional vacation rental operations. So, whether you manage 10 homes or 1,000, this series is here to give you practical frameworks, proven systems, and real-world best practices that you can apply immediately. At Breezeway, we work with thousands of property managers around the world, and every day we get to see how high-performing operators structure their team, build their workflows, and deliver five-star consistently. But, we also see where operations break down. So, in this series, housekeeping excellence, we're going to talk about what it actually takes to build and run a smooth, scalable housekeeping operation in your vacation rental business. So far, we've covered how to structure your cleaning team, how to find and retain great cleaners, and how to pay them fairly. >> But now, we're going to talk about something that brings all of that together, and that's the different types of cleans that you will complete in each property, and how to manage them effectively. Because one of the most common mistakes in vacation rental operations is treating every clean the same. When that happens, your quality suffers because not all cleans are created equal. A turnover clean between guest stays is very different than a deep clean, and both are very different than an onboarding clean that you complete when a new property enters your management portfolio. When these differences aren't clearly defined, a few things can start to happen. First, your cleaners won't know what's expected of them, and therefore they can't deliver or meet your expectations. Important tasks get skipped, and properties slowly decline in quality. Over time, this is going to show up in your guest reviews. It's going to show up in your owner satisfaction, your owner retention, and your maintenance costs. So, clear clean types create clarity for your team, and they define expectations, they improve efficiency, and they protect consistency. >> So, let's start with onboarding cleans. An onboarding clean happens when a property is first brought under your program. This might be because of an ownership change or when a house returns to a rental market after time off. This is not a standard clean. This is your opportunity to bring the property up to your company standards and establish a baseline for everything that follows. During an onboarding clean, the team is going to fully reset the home. That's going to include a deep top-to-bottom clean, but it also goes beyond that. That's when you're going to identify any existing damage. It's when you're going to organize the owner items, and you're going to make fixes to ensure that the property is fully set up for success. It's also when you document the condition of the home through notes and photos, so you have a clear record moving forward. In fact, this is a great time to take reference photos that you can use for your team in the future, so that when your cleaners are in the property, they can see a reference of how the property should look after each clean. Another key part of onboarding is inventory. This is when you're establishing what's in the home, what's missing, what needs to be purchased or restocked. This sets expectations not just for your team, but for the property owners as well. >> An onboarding clean isn't about speed. It's about being thorough and setting the standard. If you get this step right, every clean that follows becomes easier and more consistent. >> Next is your standard turnover clean. It's the most frequent and most time-sensitive type of clean in your operation. Turnover cleans happen between guest stays, and they're often performed within really tight time frames, especially during peak season or back-to-back bookings. The goal of a turnover clean is to really fully reset the property so that it's ready for the next guest, including a clean of all guest-facing areas, replacement of linens, restocking of essentials, removing trash, and checking for any visible issues or damage that may have occurred. Your turnover cleans require both speed and precision, and there's very little margin for error. Your checklist for a turnover clean should be clear, focused, and structured on the tasks that are directly impacting the guest experience. Your high-touch surfaces, your bathrooms, your kitchens, the way that the bed presentation happens, amenity restocking, and they should be visually checking for damage or any missing items. Turnover cleans is where your operation is most exposed, and if something is missed, it's likely the guest who's going to find it. That's why clarity and consistency are so important here. >> Now, let's talk about deep cleans because these go far beyond your standard turnover clean. They focus on the areas that don't often get attention during every guest day, including things like your baseboard, the vents, behind your furniture, inside of appliances, the cabinets, upholstery. Deep cleans are essential for maintaining the long-term quality of the property. Without them, even the most well-managed properties slowly start to show wear and tear over time. You might not notice it immediately, but your guests will. So, deep cleans should be scheduled intentionally. That might mean that they're done seasonally, after periods of high occupancy, or before your peak booking window begins. They can also be triggered by inspection findings if the quality starts to decline. The key is that deep cleans are planned. They are not reactive. They're going to require more time, more detail, and different expectations than a standard turnover clean. Deep cleans protect your properties and your brand over time. >> Additionally, you may have mid-stay cleans. A mid-stay clean is going to happen at some point while the guest is still in house. Sometimes this is an upsell opportunity that a guest pays for to have you come in in the middle of their trip to service the home. Other times, this may just be part of your process. Let's say that you have a guest who's in house for 30, 60 days. Maybe you want to go in at the mid-stay point to get eyes and ears in the property and sort of anticipate anything that may come up after that guest departs. Additionally, and something that we're seeing more frequently, are mini deep cleans. So, this is when somebody is breaking down all of the components of a really thorough deep clean into smaller, more palatable bites. This is going to allow you to potentially lessen the amount of time that a property is out of service because you're doing these on multiple, multiple trips. And of course, we see a lot of folks who are doing pet cleans for properties that do welcome pets because there's a different level of cleanliness that has to be taken when you've had a pet in the property. >> Now that we've defined these different types of cleans, let's talk about how you schedule them. Each type of clean is really going to require a different approach. Your turnover cleans are typically pretty straightforward because they're tied to a reservation. These are automatically scheduled based off of check-in and check-out times, so there's really not an opportunity for anything to be missed. But, your onboarding and deep cleans are going to be scheduled with more intention. This is the same for your mini deep cleans and or mid-stay cleans. Pet cleans are probably going to follow the similar frequency of your turnover cleans because they're tied to folks who have pets as part of their reservation. But, for those cleans that are are not driven by reservations, it really needs to mean that you're setting aside dedicated time, assigning the right team, and ensuring that the scope of work is clearly defined. Each clean type should have its own scope, its own checklist, and its own time allocation. >> And this is where having the right systems is paramount. With Breezeway, you can create and assign different types of tasks for each clean. For example, as I said, your turnover cleans or your pet cleanings should be scheduled automatically based off of reservations that are synced from your PMS. Your onboarding and deep cleans are going to be scheduled independently based on your operational needs and availability. Each task should have its own checklist, so that the cleaner knows exactly what's expected depending on the type of clean that they're going to be performing. Plus, your managers can then see in real time the progress and the work that is happening at each property, which removes the guesswork for your team. They don't have to wonder what type of clean they're going to be doing when they get there, or what should be done as part of their task. The system will tell them, and that clarity is going to lead to better execution. >> Before we wrap up, let's look at a few common mistakes folks can make. The first is treating your onboarding cleans like a standard turnover. Your onboarding cleans are meant to fully reset the property and establish your standards from day one. When they're rushed or treated like a quick turnover, important details are going to be missed, existing issues might go undocumented, and you lose the opportunity to set that strong baseline for all future work. The second is using the same type of checklist for each clean that's completed. When your cleaners just have one generic checklist, they either try to do too much during a turnover clean, or they miss important details of a deep clean entirely. Another is not adjusting the time or pay based on the scope of work. Each clean is going to require a different level of effort, detail, and attention, and therefore the pay needs to be reflective of that. When your expectations don't match reality, both the quality and the team morale will suffer. And if everything is treated like a turnover clean, well, then your quality is always going to suffer. The different types of work require different expectations. They require different timelines and different systems to support them. Understanding the different types of vacation rental cleans is a key step in building a strong foundation to your housekeeping operations because each clean has a specific purpose. Your onboarding cleans set the standard, your turnover cleans protect the guest experience, and your deep cleans protect the long-term quality of the property and your relationship with the owner. And when these are all clearly defined and properly scheduled, your operation is so much more predictable. It's more efficient, and you have more consistency [laughter] that you're delivering. In the next video, we're going to take this one step further and talk about how to create cleaning checklists that actually support your team and ensure that those standards are followed every time. Thanks for watching and I'll see you in the next episode of Breezeway Academy.

- How to create a vacation rental cleaning checklist that actually works | Breezeway Academy: Kind: captions Language: en Hi everyone, I'm Corina Oki, and welcome back to Breezeway Academy. >> [music] >> At Breezeway Academy, you'll learn how to build stronger, more professional vacation rental operations. So, whether you manage 10 homes or 1,000, this series is here to give you proven systems, practical frameworks, and real-world best practices that you can apply immediately. At Breezeway, we work with thousands of property managers around the world, and every day we get to see how high-performing operators structure their teams, build their workflows, and deliver five-star stays consistently. But, we also see where operations breaks down. So, in this series, Housekeeping Excellence, we're going to break down what it actually takes to build and run a smooth, scalable housekeeping operations in your vacation rental business. So far, we've covered how you structure your cleaning team, how to find and retain great cleaners, how to pay them fairly, and how to define the different types of cleans that you'll be completing. Now, we're going to bring all of that together with one of the most important tools in your operations, your checklist. Because if there's one thing that separates consistent, professional operators from reactive ones, it's having a checklist that actually works. Now, a cleaning checklist isn't just a list of tasks. It's actually how you translate your standards into something that your team can follow every time they're out in the field doing work. Without a checklist, your operation just relies on memory and your cleaners decide what clean means. So, your training can also become inconsistent. [music] And that means that the quality varies from property to property or cleaner to cleaner. But, with a strong checklist, expectations are clear and your team knows exactly what needs to be done. Your managers can easily verify the work, and your guests have the same experience every time they walk into a property under your portfolio. A checklist is the difference between hoping that a property is ready and knowing that it is. >> Every vacation rental property is different. So, how do you build a checklist that works? So, let's walk through it step-by-step. The first step is simple, but it's often skipped. Start by walking the property yourself. You need to do this alone and not quickly, not at a high level, but you need to go room by room and think through every detail from the guest's perspective. What are the high-touch areas? What are some small details that your guests will notice? What are the things that are easy to miss? If you wouldn't check it yourself, you can't expect a cleaner to catch it. And your checklist should reflect your standards. It shouldn't be based on assumptions. Next, you need to separate your checklist based off of the type of work being done. As we talked about in the last video, not all cleans are created the same, and a standard turnover clean has a very different scope than a deep clean. Your turnover cleans focus on guest-facing essentials. They're done with speed and precision, and your deep cleans are more focused on details that are less frequently checked that protect the long-term quality of the property. If you try to use one checklist for everything, you're either going to overwhelm your team or you'll miss important details. Your checklist should also reflect your brand. What does clean actually mean for your business? So, that might include how the beds are made, how the towels are folded, what supplies are placed and where, how your amenities are presented. You want to document these things down to the details, because two properties can be equally clean, but feel completely different to a guest. And your checklist is going to ensure that your standards are delivered consistently across all of your homes. Because every property is different, that means that each property should have its own custom checklist. Some will have pools, some will have hot tubs, some have unique appliances or layouts, and some owners have very specific preferences. One of the biggest mistakes operators can make is keeping that information separate from your checklist in notes or emails or, worse yet, in someone's memory. Additionally, property-specific details should be built directly into your checklist. You want to include reference photos in that checklist of exactly how the owner wants the throw pillows arranged or whatever other scenario you have in your business. But, that way, the cleaners don't have to guess and nothing gets missed, and you get fewer calls from your owners, because your owner expectations shouldn't live in someone's inbox. They should live in your system. >> Now, creating a checklist is one thing, but getting your team to regularly use it is another, and this is where many operators fall short. >> Paper checklists or static documents might work at a small scale, but as you grow, they'll break down, and they get outdated or ignored by cleaners because they become familiar with the property. Paper checklists are hard to enforce, and there's no visibility into whether the work is actually being done. A checklist only works if it's part of the workflow, not separate from it. For a checklist to work in the field, it needs to be simple, clear, and easy to follow. Tasks should be written out in plain language and follow a logical order that matches how the cleaner moves through the home. They should also remove any guesswork, not add to it. Your cleaner should be able to reference details, understand expectations, and move effectively through the task. >> And this is where digital checklists make a huge difference. With Breezeway, checklists live directly inside the task that's being created. Your cleaners access them on our mobile app while they're at a property, even if they don't have Wi-Fi. These checklists can be translated into different languages, making them accessible to diverse teams. As managers, you can include reference photos that show exactly what done should look like. And as tasks are completed, that property status is updating in real time, which turns your checklist into a working system, not just a document. But, a checklist only works if your cleaners actually use it. >> So, let's talk about accountability. Even with a strong checklist, you need to have a way for them to verify that the work was done, and this is where that documentation comes in. In Breezeway's mobile checklist, you can make it a requirement to upload photos of specific checklist items. For example, emptying the fridge or placing the amenities. And this level of documentation creates a record of what was done, what the property looked like before the next guest arrived. It's also good practice to take photos when you arrive at a property after a guest has departed, in case there are any claims after the fact. All of this protects your team and protects your business, and it reduces disputes. Photos turn a checklist from a to-do list to proof of work. >> This also ties directly into something that we covered in the last video, which is cleaner pay. One of the biggest sources of confusion is not knowing what work is being paid for. But, when checklists are tied to task completion and the task completion is tied to payment, everything becomes clearer. Your cleaners know what's expected, your managers know what was done, and the payments are based on that verified work, not guesswork. All of this reduces the back and forth, and improves trust, and it makes your operation more efficient. >> So, let's take a quick look at how this comes together in Breezeway. Each task, regardless of the type of clean, has its own checklist. Those tasks are organized by room, and we have clear instructions for each step. There are property-specific notes that are included as needed, and reference photos help the cleaners know the expected results. As the cleaner moves through the task, they can mark off the work that's already been completed and upload any documentation that's required. From a manager's perspective, you get to see what's been completed, what's still in progress, and whether or not the property is ready for the next guest. All of this creates so much more visibility across your entire organization. >> Before we wrap up, let's look at a few common mistakes. One is overloading your checklist with too many tasks. When a checklist becomes too long or it's overly detailed, your cleaners might rush through it or they start to ignore parts of it altogether. A good checklist should be thorough, but also practical and usable in the time allotted. Another mistake is using the same checklist for every property. Different homes have different needs, and your checklist should reflect those differences. A one-size-fits-all approach leads to missed details and poor quality. We also see operators creating checklists, but never updating them. As your standards evolve, as there are upgrades to the property made, or as owners buy new furniture, your checklist should update along with those improvements. Otherwise, your systems just outdated. And finally, not reviewing checklist completion regularly. If managers aren't checking that the tasks are being completed properly, then accountability breaks down. A checklist that no one looks at is just busy work. Creating a strong cleaning checklist is one of the most important steps that you can take to build a scalable housekeeping operation. It turns your standards into systems, and it gives your team clarity. Plus, it gives you visibility into what's happening across your entire portfolio. And more importantly, it ties everything that we've covered in this series together. Choosing the right team, hiring and training the right cleaners, paying them fairly, defining the different types of work that's done, and now creating a system that ensures it all gets done the right way. Housekeeping excellence isn't just about working harder. It's about building systems that work for you. Feel free to drop a comment below if you have any questions, or click the link below to book time with our team of experts. Thanks for joining us for this Housekeeping Excellence series of Breezeway Academy, and we'll see you in the next one.
