  18. @HullAYR: Building and planning a wellness resort is an exciting venture! Here are some key factors to consider:

Location and Accessibility:

Choose a serene environment that promotes relaxation.
Ensure easy access to transportation and nearby attractions.
Design and Layout:

Create a harmonious flow between indoor and outdoor spaces.
Incorporate natural elements like water features and gardens.
Treatment and Service Offerings:

Provide a variety of wellness treatments (spa, yoga, nutrition).
Consider the expertise of staff and the quality of services.
Sustainability:

Use eco-friendly materials and practices.
Implement energy-efficient systems to minimize environmental impact.
Target Audience:

Identify your target demographic (families, couples, solo travelers).
Tailor services and marketing strategies to meet their needs.
Amenities and Facilities:

Include fitness centers, pools, and relaxation areas.
Offer healthy dining options that align with wellness goals.
Regulatory Compliance:

Ensure adherence to local regulations and health standards.
Obtain necessary permits and licenses for operation.
Marketing and Branding:

Develop a strong brand identity that reflects your wellness philosophy.
Utilize digital marketing strategies to reach potential guests.

By focusing on these aspects, you can create a wellness resort that not only attracts guests but also provides them with a rejuvenating experience. What specific area are you most interested in exploring further? 😊
Practically: 
Things to consider when building and planning for a wellness resort and retreat
Aria: 
You're on the right track! Building a wellness resort and retreat requires careful planning. Here are some key things to consider:

1.  Target Audience & Niche:

Who are you attracting? Families, couples, solo travelers, specific age groups, those seeking specific wellness practices?
What sets you apart?  Focus on a unique niche, like yoga retreats, mindfulness programs, detox programs, or specific health conditions.

2. Location & Environment:

Nature's Embrace: Choose a location with natural beauty - mountains, beaches, forests - that promotes relaxation and tranquility.
Accessibility:  Consider ease of access for guests, transportation options, and proximity to airports or train stations.

3.  Wellness Programs & Services:

Holistic Approach:  Offer a variety of wellness programs that address physical, mental, and emotional well-being.
Expert Staff:  Hire qualified and experienced professionals in areas like yoga, massage, nutrition, meditation, and fitness.

4.  Design & Architecture:

Harmony with Nature:  Integrate natural elements like wood, stone, and water features to create a serene atmosphere.
Spacious & Comfortable:  Ensure rooms and communal areas are spacious, well-lit, and promote relaxation.

5.  Sustainability & Eco-Consciousness:

Environmental Responsibility:  Use sustainable building materials, renewable energy sources, and minimize waste.
Local Sourcing:  Support local businesses and farmers for food and products.

6.  Technology & Amenities:

Modern Amenities:  Offer high-speed internet, comfortable beds, and modern bathrooms.
Technology Integration:  Consider incorporating technology for booking, communication, and wellness tracking.

7.  Marketing & Branding:

Unique Identity:  Develop a compelling brand story and marketing strategy that resonates with your target audience.
Digital Presence:  Establish a strong online presence through a website, social media, and online booking platforms.

8.  Financial Planning:

Investment & Budget:  Develop a realistic budget and financial plan that accounts for construction, operational costs, and marketing.
Return on Investment:  Consider the potential return on investment and long-term sustainability of the resort.

great care of their last property they'll probably take great care of mine and that seems to be how it's working out for me so pretty simple and I really honestly like being able to select my own had a lot higher turnover and just tenants that weren't taking great care of the property so I don't really know what was going wrong there but I've had much better life selecting my own tenants number two maintenance and repairs now I think this is where a lot of new landlords get scared because they assume that they're gonna have to be handling maintenance requests all the time and that's just really not the case so I have like I said 16 properties and even with that number of properties which I know it's not a huge amount but with even with 16 I really probably get one phone call or text every two months concerning something that needs to be repaired so it's not like I'm getting a call all you know every week or every day it's really very infrequent and when I do it's just not a big deal at all so for example the very last call that I got a tenant called me and said that in his shower beat hot water when he would turn it off it wouldn't drip it wasn't shutting all the way off and so I was like okay no problem I'm gonna call the plumber and I will give him your contact information so he can get in touch with you and set up a time to come fix up my tenant says okay great thank you very much I call the plumber it's the plumber that I use every time we have a plumbing problem so he knows me and we have a really good relationship I just give him my tenants number and he goes out there and fixes it and then he just shoots me a picture of the invoice and I send him a check in the mail so it works really well and that's the relationship that I've built with each of the different contractors that we use so that's the plumber but we also have a guy that I use for like an electrician that we use for electrical problems a heat and air guy roofer a guy that works on septic tanks and cleans out you know pumps out the septic tanks and so I have a good relationship with all of the people we've used because number one I pay them on time and that's gonna be your biggest thing and and number two I'm a pretty loyal customer and as long as they do a great job for me I'm gonna use that same person every time so for me having a property manager as a middleman just isn't necessary it's it's really simple to handle maintenance request as long as you have a good relationship with the contractors that you use and also it gives me the option to fix it myself if I want to for example a couple months ago a tenant their toilet handle broke I know how to replace the toilet handle and it cost about five dollars to buy a new toilet handle at Walmart literally you go buy the handle unscrew the old one screw in the new one connect it to the little flap thing and you have a brand new handle and it works great so for five dollars I fixed that myself whereas with a property manager I probably would have had to pay more to have somebody else go over there and do it anyway I like being able to handle the repair and maintenance request myself and it's not a headache at all to do so number three collecting rent I think collecting rent should be about the least stressful thing you do and for me it is I don't ever hardly ever have to think about it I collect rent online using cozy Co and the only negative to that is it takes five business days to process the payments so you're probably gonna be getting your rent about a week later than you would be if you would just go collect it in person but you don't have to deal with going to collect it in person you don't have to deal with checks that might bounce you don't have to deal having your renters mailing the check and saying it got lost in the mail or bringing it to you it's just really easy the late fees are automatic which is nice because I have a hard time telling people oh yeah you have to pay $35 more it's a lot easier if the computer program just tells them that but possibly the only time not maybe I would have an issue is if somebody just stopped paying even then I don't know that the property manager could do a lot for that but what I do is just go to my attorney and say hey this person has to be evicted will you handle that for me and it's not I know it's not something exceptions to that rule but as a rule a thumb don't charge more than one month's rent you can only deduct normal wear and tear and so some owners are very liberal with what's normal wear and tear better financial decision because it takes four to six months sometimes to evict a tenant and then you got the attorney's fees and then you'll have this judgment where the tenant has to pay and you ain't never ever seeing that money 80% of the time all right so routine preventative maintenance here's a few good tips put in your lease that the tenants required to change the HVAC filter and then a leak happening you want to test the smoke alarms make sure they didn't dismantle them make sure there's a CO2 alarm check your water heater see how old it is if it's like 10 years old you might want to replace it proactively because they tend to fail after 10 years and they can leak you want to do an exterior walk around the property just make sure the drainage is good make sure there's no issues with trees on the house where rats can jump on and termite damage you getting a termite fumigation every 7 to 10 years at a minimum is a good idea and then there's a couple good tips with tenants I like to ask hey have you fixed anything or upgraded anything since you've been here because sometimes we don't remember exactly what the house looked like and they'll usually confess they'll say like oh yeah I actually did this I painted this wall or I installed this ceiling fan another good one is to check that your key still works sometimes they'll change the locks and you don't know so you want always want to make sure your key is working now make sure you establish an emergency maintenance protocol with your tenant put it in your lease or in your welcome information to your tenant in the email how do they reach you after hours if they don't reach you do they have a number for a plumber or who should they call what if you're on vacation and you don't answer so make sure they know who to call the last thing you want is some Plum Plumbing emergency or something and you're not reachable they might call just like some random ad and it's this plumber that's five times more expensive than like a normal plumber and that's if you're lucky and on your inspection also you're just looking for safety hazards like tripping hazards is the fence need to be replaced or can someone fall off this railing flooring just making sure things are in good condition So Pro tip how are we going to pay for these repairs and maintenance at the beginning we're going to set up a separate bank account for the rental property so that makes tax time much easier all the money comes in and out for the property and then what we're going to do is we're going to set aside at least three months expenses into that account that's going to pay for future things and then we're going to replenish it we're going to assume our property needs maintenance our car needs maintenance and we usually understand that but oftentimes we bunk ourselves in the head and say why do we have to fix this oh this is broken things break down they're going to need to be fixed it's a good rule of thumb just to allocate maybe 10% of rent each month just to keep in that account or uh just try to build up the rent and not even use it to to pay for future expenses down the road so it's a good rule of thumb to keep a 3 to six Monon buffer in the account and that way you can pay for future expenses you've got money allocated for it and it's less frustrating when you actually have to pay for it all right so in the unlikely event you need to evict your tenant here's how the eviction process works at a high level in California so first off there's only two reasons you can really evict a tenant one is non-payment of rent and the second is an egregious lease violation like I'm not talking about they hung a towel on the balcony and the is mad and this is the third time it's happened I'm talking about like they are a threat to people or property they've substantially damaged your property or they're a major nuisance to your community and it's all documented you better have a really good case because judges don't want to hear about your lease violation eviction most of the time I guess there's one other reason that could be an eviction if the tenant doesn't move out at the end of the lease term you know they were supposed to move out and they didn't all right so they didn't pay the rent it's the most common one you serve the 3-day notice to pay in the example of the lease violation you'd serve the 3-day notice to cure violation or quit and in the example of the person that didn't move out at the end of the lease term you would just serve an unconditional notice to quit and move out you wait the three days then it's time to file your unlawful detainer with your attorney you'll get a court date both parties will get to go to court they will ask you before you go into court if you will please mediate both of you will say no and then you will go to court just kidding usually landlords are open to mediate but often times tenants don't want to mediate they want their day in court so you'll have the opportunity present your case come very prepared bring all of your evidence in a nice binder that's all labeled make the judge's life super easy if you win you'll get a rid of possession you'll get a judgment often if they owe you money that judgment you can enforce with a collection company but oftentimes it's very hard to get money from tenants that needed to be evicted you get the share of lockout date you'll show up then you'll get your property back hopefully most first-time landlords greatly underestimate the time and energy it takes to manage rental property to actually do - How to Manage Your Rental Properties Efficiently: Expert Tips for Success: Kind: captions Language: en managing rental properties isn't as easy as it sounds the reality is that owning rental properties comes with a host of challenges from dealing with tenants to ensuring your properties are always in top condition if you're not managing things efficiently your investment could quickly turn into a money pit so how can you avoid the most common mistakes and ensure your properties generate consistent passive income the secret lies in smart Strategic Management the problem timec consuming stressful management many landlords struggle with keeping up with property maintenance tenant communication rent collection and the countless other tasks that come with managing rental properties this can lead to burnout lost revenue and headaches that make you question if it's all worth it however the truth is with the right strategies you can manage your properties with ease and increase your profits expert tips for efficient Property Management streamline tenant communication effective communication is key to a successful rental business use property management software or app to streamline tenant applications lease agreements and maintenance requests this keeps everything organized and ensures you never miss an important deadline or request automate rent collection one of the most common problems landlords face is late rent payments avoid the hassle of chasing down tenants by automating the rent collection process there are various payment platforms that allow tenants to pay online making it easier to track payments and reduce the risk of missed rent set clear expectations one way to to prevent misunderstandings is by setting clear written expectations for both you and your tenants a well-drafted lease agreement should outline everything from payment schedules to maintenance responsibilities this reduces potential conflicts and helps keep everything running smoothly regular property inspections regularly inspect your properties to ensure everything is in good condition catching small issues early can save you from expensive repairs later schedule inspections every 6 months or after tenants move out to maintain the value of your property build a reliable maintenance team having a reliable team of contractors and maintenance professionals is essential when an issue arises having a trusted team in place ensures quick repairs and minimizes downtime this way you can ensure your property remains in top shape and your tenants stay happy the secret to success systematize your processes the key to efficiently managing rental properties is to create systems by automating tasks communicating clearly and staying organized you'll save time reduce stress and increase your bottom line the more streamlined your processes are the easier it becomes to manage multiple properties without feeling overwhelmed

- Free Education for Street-Begging Kids: Tech Leader’s Inspiring NGO Story #socialimpact  #education: Kind: captions Language: en Chuk education is a foundation provide free education for school dropouts especially for the children who are begging in the streets and doing labor activities. When a child goes for labor activities, we need to stop them from going there and we want them to come to the school. We try to bargain with the family and we said that okay we'll give you 1,000 rupees for groceries. As a return you give your child to our learning center. So I think there's a madan movie where this kind of a bargaining system happens. So the only way that how we take all these challenges is see it as a challenge not like a problem. If you see it as a challenge it will give you the curiosity to figure out what can be the best answer for resolving the challenge. [Music] What if your first words each morning were please for coins instead of good morning teacher. For thousands of street children, that's life. Waking before dawn, clutching metal bowls, spreading through traffic for spare change, where classrooms stay locked and lessons feel impossible. Welcome to stories within. I'm your host Husti and today we are joined by Yua, co-founder of Shuki Education. She refused to let begging define these kids' future and turn sidewalks into actual schools. Armed with donated notebooks and handful of committed college student volunteers, US team set up open air classrooms in bustling market lanes. The toughest battle was in rain or traffic. It was convincing parents that trading a few coins for few hours of study could change everything. They invited parents to sit on trial lessons, showed how even 30 minutes of reading aloud builds confidence, and adjusted class times around children's earning schedule so families never lost income. Through neighborhood meetings, door-to-door outreach, and sharing early success stories, UA scaled from one small session to hundreds of enrolled learners, kids who now write their names and their own destiny. In this episode, Yua will reveal the moment she realized charity needed a classroom. How she won over skeptical parents to let their childrens learn. The fundraising and volunteer recruitment hacks that kept the program running. Hit subscribe because Chic Education proves that with strategy, persistence, and a community willing to rethink charity, even the roughest payment can become a place of possibility. Let's dive in. a very warm welcome and I'm extremely honored to have you here with us today. Thank you for joining us. >> Thank you so much Husti for uh inviting me. I am equally honored as well. Looking forward to have a good conversation with you. >> Same here you are. Can you tell me what is Chto Education and what's your role in this organization? >> Sure. Choto education is a foundation which has been started in the year 2016 uh by me and another co-founder which goes by the name Madavi. So we both wanted to do or I can say provide free education for school dropouts especially for the children who are uh begging in the streets and doing labor activities and with this objective we started this foundation especially with a vision that to ensure all kids receive the right to education and it has been started in Hyderabad Hyderabad and it's in almost 9 years and uh we are still running the program. Initially it was only a volunteer or I would say just an initiative. It was not even registered and then in the year 2019 we have registered it officially as a section 8 company and uh we also transformed our foundation from a volunteer model to a teacher full-time employment kind of a model. We have currently four learning centers with uh one teacher at every learning center taking care of minimum 20 to 25 school dropouts. So as a first step we first identify the dropouts and try to enroll them in this educational program and uh we provide minimum one year bridging course and once that is completed we try to join them in the nearby school in order to ensure that there's a sustainability in the education. If they continue over there, good for them. But if there is going to be a dropout again happening, we will again take back them into our program and see what is the best that we can do to ensure the dropout doesn't happen again or >> Wow, that's amazing. law if the house owner needs the house for themsel if it is necessary to repair and maintain the house by removing inhabitants from the house if the rented house does not appear to be appropriate for paying rent for 3 months or more without notice and even if the tenant successor or agent cannot be found then the house owner shall submit an application with such statement to the concerned W office then the concern W office may open and backend the rented house

- How Owner Payouts Actually Work (For STR Managers With 1–10 Listings): Kind: captions Language: en If you manage between one and 10 short-term rental listings, owner payouts are usually the part of the business that creates the most stress. And it's rarely because the math is complicated. It's because of the responsibility, timing, and potential risk. You're handling money that belongs to someone else, and you want to be sure you're doing it right. Not to mention, you're trying to get it done as fast as possible, all while feeling questions from owners about where their money is. That concern and stress is completely normal at this stage. Owner payouts are where bookkeeping, compliance, and trust accounting all come together. When payouts are clear and consistent, a lot of other things in the business start to feel easier to manage, even beyond bookkeeping. That's what this video is about. Let's level set what an owner payout is, because this is where things often feel more complicated than they need to be. At its simplest, an owner payout is the end result of the process of collecting rental income, subtracting the agreed upon expenses and fees, and sending that remaining amount to the property owner with clear documentation. That's it. There's nothing inherently complex about that process when it's handled correctly, documented, and not left to the last minute. But of course, as with anything in the STR industry, nothing is quite as simple as it appears. Where things start to get messy is when parts of that process are unclear, rushed, or living only in your head. Here's the most important thing to understand. Not all the money that hits your bank account belongs to your business. Some of it belongs to owners, some to the government, even if it passes through your account first. Your role as the manager is to track that money accurately and move it to the owner in a timely and documented way. Let me walk you through what this usually looks like in real life. A guest stays at the property and pays Airbnb. That money lands in your bank account, hopefully one designated as your trust account where all deposits land. And of course, since this is Airbnb, it could be mixed in with other deposits for other reservations and other properties. But at the end of the month, you prepare the owner statement, income, expenses, management fees, and the owner share. Once that statement is finalized, the accounting part is basically done. Now you're just paying someone what they're owed. You look at the statement, you see the amount, you send the money. There should be a clear, easy to audit line from guest payment to the owner payout. If that line is hard to follow, that's usually where stress starts creeping in and where owner questions pile up. So, how are owners commonly paid at this stage? This is the part most managers want spelled out. At the 1 to 10 listing stage, there's usually a few ways this happens. Some managers log into their business bank account and send an a transfer. Some might use their bank's bill pay feature. Others might use zel or Venmo early on because they're fast and familiar, especially before things get more formal. All these can work, although we don't recommend Venmo for owner payouts for too long. stick to an a payment from your bank or from QBO if you're using that. What matters more than the tool is this. That money comes from your business bank account. The payment matches the owner statement and you can see when and where it was sent. Can't tell you the number of times we've onboarded clients. They've given us their prior month's owner statements. We can't actually match them up with the payments in the bank account. If all those things are true, the method itself matters a lot less. As you start to mature, dedicated payable platforms start to make a lot more sense. One thing that surprises a lot of managers is how early payout start meeting structure. At one or two listings, you can usually keep things pretty manual, but as you get closer to that 10 listing mark, there's more money moving around, more owners involved, more timing to keep straight. That's usually when payouts start working better with some kind of system behind them. That's why tools like Top Key, VR Trust, and Clearing exist. They're built to help once things aren't simple anymore. Some managers use those tools directly. Others keep their setup simpler, but bring in help to manage payouts. Either way, the goal is the same. You want those owner payouts to feel steady and predictable, not like something you have to double check every time. Another thing managers tend to overthink is timing. Should you pay your owners per stay, monthly? Should you pay them when you receive the cash or when the stay occurs? Again, keep this simple. Monthly payouts are industry standard and you shouldn't have to deviate from this. You should pay in a rears, which means you pay the owners for activity in the prior month. This gives you time to account for expenses and reduce mistakes. Most homeowners really care more about knowing when they'll get paid, and predictability goes a long way towards credibility, especially when you're starting out. Additionally, I strongly recommend paying owners based either on the check-in or checkout date as opposed to the cash received date. If you go with the check-in method, for example, every reservation with a check-in date during the past month would be on the owner statement. This means you don't need to worry about tracking multiple payments for a Verbbo reservation or worrying a guest will cancel and you'll need to refund money you already paid to an owner. Here's a simple way to sanity check your payout process. If an owner can look at their payout statement and understand it without you needing to explain it, your process is working at this stage. If they can't, that's usually where questions, stress, and follow-ups start. If you're really getting a lot of questions, make a simple Loom video that walks them through that process. That can go again a long way towards alleviating some of those questions. So, let's talk about where owner payouts go off the tracks. Most payout problems don't start as big mistakes. They start as small shortcuts. Paying owners from the wrong account just this once. Sending money before every booking has been reconciled. not keeping a clear record because h I'll remember it later. In practice, what this looks like is an endless stream of adjustments in the following month to fix errors that the owner flagged after reviewing the statements. Nothing destroys destroys confidence like having to claw back money the owner thought was theirs. These issues usually come from system gaps, not bad intentions. And the good news is system gaps can be fixed. So, the setup works for a while, then payouts start taking longer than they should. All of a sudden, you're missing date night or kids game because owner statements have to get done. Believe me, we do over 2500 of these a month. I get it. You start thinking you'd rather be doing anything else other than this. You start taking shortcuts, not ensuring all reservations are fully paid, missing expenses, and now your bottom line is impacted. That's usually the sign the system has outgrown you. This is one of the most common places we help at Keystone. We handle the bookkeeping, produce clear owner reports, and manage bill pays so owner payouts are consistent, documented, and easy to explain. Not because managers can't do this themselves, but because this is often the first thing people want off their plate once there's enough going on. If you're automatically rejecting any plans in your personal life during the first five days of the month, that might be a sign you need help. So, if you're managing one to 10 listings, this is what owner payouts are supposed to look like. Clear, consistent, explainable. In the next video, we'll break down which short-term rental fees you should be keeping as the manager, which should be passed through to owners, and how to structure those decisions so they're clear, easy to explain, and set you up to scale.

Finance सबरेडिट के एक रेडिट पोस्ट से। यह एक प्लेबुक है कि आपको अपने पैसों के साथ क्या करना चाहिए, जिसे मैं अपनी कमेंट्री के साथ समझाऊंगा। तो चलिए सीधे स्टेप नंबर एक से शुरू करते हैं। एक हाई इनकम अर्नर के तौर पर, आपके पैसे सबसे पहले एक इमरजेंसी फंड बनाने में जाने चाहिए, जिसमें कम से कम तीन से छह महीने के खर्चे हों। रेडिट गाइड सलाह देती है कि आप अपने छह महीने के खर्चे एक हाई यील्ड सेविंग्स अकाउंट या अकाउंट, यानी कैश मैनेजमेंट अकाउंट पर विचार कर सकते हैं। ये सारे ऑप्शन्स एक जैसे ही हैं। वे चाहते हैं कि आप अपना कैश मनी मार्केट फंड या किसी लिक्विड जगह पर रखें जहाँ आपको 2.5 से 5% का इंटरेस्ट मिले। छह महीने के इमरजेंसी फंड की सलाह काफी अच्छी है। तो, एक हाई अर्नर के तौर पर, अगर आप साल का $150k या उससे ज़्यादा कमा रहे हैं, तो आपका लाइफस्टाइल और खर्चे शायद ज़्यादा होंगे। आप शायद घर की मॉर्गेज, ज़्यादा इंश्योरेंस प्रीमियम, एक अच्छी कार और बच्चों की ट्यूशन फीस दे रहे हों। एक हाई अर्नर के तौर पर नौकरी जाने पर, खर्चों में तुरंत कटौती मुश्किल होती है। इसलिए, जितना ज़्यादा कमाते हैं, इमरजेंसी फंड उतना ही बड़ा होना चाहिए। ट्रेज़री ETF या कैश मैनेजमेंट अकाउंट का सुझाव अच्छा है, क्योंकि HENRYs अक्सर बड़े कैश बैलेंस को रेगुलर सेविंग्स अकाउंट में छोड़ देते हैं, जहाँ सिर्फ ज़ीरो पॉइंट एक या ज़ीरो पॉइंट ज़ीरो ज़ीरो एक परसेंट मिलता है। जबकि वे उसी सेफ्टी और लिक्विडिटी के साथ ढाई से पाँच परसेंट कमा सकते थे। पचास हज़ार डॉलर के इमरजेंसी फंड पर यह एक्स्ट्रा यील्ड हर साल लगभग दो हज़ार डॉलर अतिरिक्त हो सकती है। यहाँ सबसे ज़रूरी पहलू हैं लिक्विडिटी बनाए रखना और साथ ही अपने इमरजेंसी फंड से पैसे भी कमाना। ठीक है, स्टेप नंबर दो, दूसरी जगह जहाँ आपके पैसे जाने चाहिए वो है आपके HSA कॉन्ट्रिब्यूशन्स को मैक्स करना, अगर आप एलिजिबल हैं तो। HSA बस एक हेल्थ सेविंग्स अकाउंट होता है। और इस अकाउंट में आप इन्वेस्ट कर सकते हैं और क्वालिफाइड मेडिकल खर्चों के लिए पेमेंट भी कर सकते हैं। अब, इस आर्टिकल ने इसे प्रायोरिटी लिस्ट में नंबर दो पर क्यों रखा है, इसके कुछ कारण हैं। पहला, HSA सबसे ज़्यादा टैक्स एफिशिएंट सेविंग्स व्हीकल है क्योंकि यह ट्रिपल टैक्स बेनिफिट्स देता है। नंबर एक, आपके कॉन्ट्रिब्यूशन्स टैक्स डिडक्टिबल होते हैं, तो जिस भी साल आप इसमें कॉन्ट्रिब्यूट करते हैं, यह आपके टोटल इनकम टैक्स को कम कर देता है। नंबर दो, इस अकाउंट की ग्रोथ टैक्स फ्री होती है, बिल्कुल Roth IRA की तरह। और नंबर तीन, क्वालिफाइड मेडिकल खर्चों के लिए विथड्रॉअल्स भी टैक्स फ्री होते हैं। HSA के बारे में सबसे दिलचस्प बात यह है कि आज अपनी जेब से किए गए मेडिकल खर्चे के लिए आप भविष्य में खुद को रीइम्बर्स कर सकते हैं। तो मान लीजिए कि आज आपको दो हज़ार डॉलर का एक मेडिकल खर्चा देना है। HSA फंड्स इस्तेमाल करने के बजाय, अच्छी इनकम होने पर आप इसे अपनी जेब से दे सकते हैं। इससे HSA फंड्स टैक्स फ्री ग्रो करते हैं और पिछले मेडिकल खर्चों के लिए पैसे एक्सेस करने का ऑप्शन भी रहता है। तो जब तक आप रसीद संभाल कर रखते हैं, मान लीजिए कि बीस साल बाद, आपके HSA में रखे दो हज़ार डॉलर बढ़कर आठ हज़ार डॉलर हो गए हैं। आप असल में पूरे आठ हज़ार डॉलर निकाल सकते हैं और बीस साल पुराने मेडिकल खर्चे का इस्तेमाल करके इसे टैक्स फ्री विथड्रॉअल के लिए क्वालिफाई कर सकते हैं, जो काफी पावरफुल है। HSA के इस सुझाव पर मेरा मानना है कि यह बिल्कुल सही है। मैं कहूँगा कि यह स्टेप अगले स्टेप के साथ इंटरचेंजेबल हो सकता है, जो कि 401k और IRA जैसे अकाउंट्स को मैक्स आउट करना है। लेकिन कुछ लोग इसे प्रायोरिटाइज़ करना चाहेंगे, खासकर अगर उनका एम्प्लॉयर HSA में कुछ कॉन्ट्रिब्यूशन्स को मैच करता है। यह उन लोगों के लिए भी अच्छा है जो अपनी हेल्थ के हिसाब से एक हेल्थकेयर इमरजेंसी फंड चाहते हैं। अगर आपके पास HSA है तो एक बात जो आपको पक्की करनी है वो यह है कि आप असल में फंड्स को इन्वेस्ट कर रहे हैं। बहुत से लोग बस एक HSA या Roth IRA शुरू कर देते हैं और वे कोई भी फंड इन्वेस्ट नहीं करते, वे बस उसमें पैसे जमा करते हैं और उसका कुछ नहीं करते। तो यह पक्का करें कि आप उस पैसे को लो-कॉस्ट इंडेक्स फंड्स में डाल रहे हैं और इस तरह आप कम से कम HSA की टैक्स एफिशिएंसी का फायदा उठा पाएंगे। स्टेप नंबर तीन रिटायरमेंट कॉन्ट्रिब्यूशन्स के बारे में है। मतलब अपने 401k, IRA या Roth IRAs में कॉन्ट्रिब्यूट करना। हमारे चैनल के सिक्सटी-सेवन परसेंट दर्शक US में हैं, जहाँ एम्प्लॉयर स्पॉन्सर्ड 401k प्लान्स होते हैं। अगर आप UK में हैं, तो वहाँ वर्कप्लेस पेंशन होती है। ऑस्ट्रेलिया में इसे सुपरएन्युएशन कहते हैं। कनाडा में RRSPs होते हैं। किसी भी हाल में, आप अपनी सैलरी का एक परसेंट रिटायरमेंट फंड में बचाना चाहेंगे। कई एक्सपर्ट्स का सुझाव है कि अपने पेचेक का लगभग दस से पंद्रह परसेंट रिटायरमेंट के लिए अलग रखें। यह सुनिश्चित करता है कि आप आराम से रिटायर हो पाएंगे। अमेरिका में एम्प्लॉयर मैचेस होते हैं। अगर आपका एम्प्लॉयर 401k मैच ऑफर करता है, तो हमेशा उसका पूरा फायदा उठाएँ। इसे समझाने के लिए, मान लीजिए आप साल का एक लाख डॉलर कमाते हैं। आपका एम्प्लॉयर हर साल आपकी सैलरी का तीन परसेंट तक या आपके तीन हज़ार डॉलर के कॉन्ट्रिब्यूशन्स को 401k में मैच कर सकता है। आप इसका फायदा उठाना चाहेंगे क्योंकि यह एम्प्लॉयर की तरफ से फ्री का पैसा है। अमेरिका में एक हायर अर्नर के तौर पर, आपको यह देखना चाहिए कि क्या आपका एम्प्लॉयर मेगा बैकडोर कॉन्ट्रिब्यूशन ऑफर करता है। यह आपको तेईस हज़ार पाँच सौ डॉलर प्रति वर्ष की नॉर्मल कॉन्ट्रिब्यूशन लिमिट से ज़्यादा कॉन्ट्रिब्यूट करने देता है। हाल ही में, कई कंपनियाँ इसे ऑफर करने लगी हैं, खासकर टेक फील्ड में जहाँ गूगल, माइक्रोसॉफ्ट, मेटा और अमेज़न सभी इसे ऑफर करते हैं। मैं अभी स्क्रीन पर उनकी पूरी लिस्ट छोड़ दूँगा जिसका आप स्क्रीनशॉट ले सकते हैं, लेकिन मैं नीचे इस रिसोर्स का एक लिंक भी दूँगा जिसमें उन कई अलग-अलग कंपनियों की लिस्ट है जो मेगा बैकडोर रॉथ ऑफर करती हैं। आमतौर पर, यह ऐसे काम करता है कि आप नॉर्मल कॉन्ट्रिब्यूशन लिमिट से ज़्यादा अपने प्लान में दिए गए एक आफ्टर-टैक्स बकेट में कॉन्ट्रिब्यूट करेंगे। फिर आप अपने 401k के अंदर ही रॉथ में इन-प्लान कन्वर्जन करेंगे या उसे रॉथ IRA में रोल कर देंगे। कॉन्ट्रिब्यूशन्स पर पहले ही टैक्स देने से, इस कन्वर्जन पर टैक्स नहीं लगेगा, और यह बहुत पावरफुल है। अगर कन्फ्यूजिंग लग रहा है, तो एक अमेज़न एम्प्लॉई का उदाहरण देखते हैं जो साल का तीन लाख पैंतालीस हज़ार डॉलर कमाता है। उन्हें पूरा एम्प्लॉयर मैच मिलता है और वे अपने मेगा बैकडोर कॉन्ट्रिब्यूशन में और भी ज़्यादा कॉन्ट्रिब्यूट कर पाते हैं। आप देख सकते हैं कि अगर आपकी उम्र पचास साल से कम है, तो आप इस अकाउंट में हर साल उनहत्तर हज़ार डॉलर तक कॉन्ट्रिब्यूट कर सकते हैं, जिसमें मैच भी शामिल है। और यह समय के साथ वेल्थ जेनरेट करने के लिए बेहद पावरफुल है। तो हाँ, अपने रिटायरमेंट अकाउंट्स में कॉन्ट्रिब्यूट करने के अलावा, यह ज़रूर देखें कि क्या आपकी कंपनी किसी भी तरह का मेगा बैकडोर ऑफर करती है। इसके अलावा, बस यह पक्का करें कि आप अपने रिटायरमेंट अकाउंट में लगातार और नियमित रूप से कॉन्ट्रिब्यूट और इन्वेस्ट कर रहे हैं, क्योंकि स्टेप तीन आपकी नेट वर्थ बढ़ाने के लिए बहुत ज़रूरी है। ठीक है, स्टेप चार है पाँच परसेंट या उससे ज़्यादा इंटरेस्ट रेट वाले कर्ज़ चुकाना। मुझे यह बात बहुत पसंद है, क्योंकि इस स्टेप में मन की शांति का भी ध्यान रखा गया है। पहला, रेडिट आर्टिकल कहता है कि अगर आप सेविंग्स खत्म करने का सोच रहे हैं, तो ऐसा सिर्फ दस परसेंट से ज़्यादा इंटरेस्ट रेट वाले कर्ज़ों के लिए करें। पाँच से छह परसेंट इंटरेस्ट रेट वाले कर्ज़ों के लिए, सेविंग्स खत्म करने के बजाय लिक्विडिटी बनाए रखना और एक्स्ट्रा पेमेंट्स करना बेहतर है। दस परसेंट से ज़्यादा वाले कर्ज़ चुकाने पर ROI इतना अच्छा होता है कि सेविंग्स इस्तेमाल करना फायदेमंद है। जब यह पाँच परसेंट के करीब आता है, तब आप उन कर्ज़ों को पूरा चुकाने के लिए अपना समय ले सकते हैं। आपके मॉर्गेज की बात करें तो, आर्टिकल कहता है कि अगर आप डिडक्शन्स आइटमाइज कर रहे हैं, तो सात लाख पचास हज़ार डॉलर से कम के मॉर्गेज को प्रीपे न करें। यह सलाह तभी सही है जब मॉर्गेज सात लाख पचास हज़ार डॉलर से कम हो, आप डिडक्शन्स आइटमाइज कर रहे हों और इंटरेस्ट रेट इतना कम हो कि इन्वेस्टिंग, मॉर्गेज चुकाने से बेहतर रिटर्न दे। जो लोग नहीं जानते, US में सात लाख पचास हज़ार डॉलर से कम बैलेंस वाले मॉर्गेज पर आप चुकाए गए सारे इंटरेस्ट को डिडक्ट कर सकते हैं, बशर्ते आप डिडक्शन्स को आइटमाइज कर रहे हों। आपको खुद पता लगाना होगा कि क्या आपके सिनेरियो में आइटमाइज्ड डिडक्शन्स, सरकार के स्टैंडर्ड डिडक्शन से बेहतर हैं। अगर आप मैरिड फाइलिंग जॉइंटली कर रहे हैं, तो दो हज़ार पच्चीस में आपका स्टैंडर्ड डिडक्शन इकत्तीस हज़ार पाँच सौ डॉलर है। इस मॉर्गेज इंटरेस्ट डिडक्शन से तभी फायदा होगा जब आपके टोटल आइटमाइज्ड डिडक्शन्स स्टैंडर्ड डिडक्शन से ज़्यादा हों। एक उदाहरण के तौर पर, मान लीजिए आप शादीशुदा हैं और आपके पास मॉर्गेज इंटरेस्ट पच्चीस हज़ार, स्टेट और लोकल टैक्स दस हज़ार, और चैरिटेबल डोनेशन पाँच हज़ार हैं। कुल आइटमाइज्ड डिडक्शन चालीस हज़ार डॉलर हुए। क्योंकि चालीस हज़ार, इकत्तीस हज़ार पाँच सौ से ज़्यादा है, आपको टैक्स बचाने के लिए आइटमाइज करना चाहिए और फिर देखें कि स्टेप चार आप पर लागू होता है या नहीं। स्टेप चार में, हाई इंटरेस्ट वाले कर्ज चुकाना आपकी पहली प्रायोरिटी होनी चाहिए। अगर आपके पास दस परसेंट से ऊपर कुछ भी है, तो यहीं से शुरू करना बेस्ट है। स्टेप नंबर पाँच है एक टैक्सेबल ब्रोकरेज अकाउंट में इन्वेस्ट करना। अगर आप हाई अर्नर हैं और स्टेप एक से चार तक कर चुके हैं, तो एक्स्ट्रा सेविंग्स लॉन्ग टर्म ग्रोथ के लिए टैक्सेबल ब्रोकरेज अकाउंट में जा सकती है। किसमें इन्वेस्ट करना है, गाइड आपको वीटीआई और वीओओ जैसे ईटीएफ में इन्वेस्ट करने की सलाह देती है, न कि इंडिविजुअल स्टॉक्स में। इंडिविजुअल स्टॉक्स ज़्यादा रिटर्न के कारण रोमांचक हो सकते हैं, पर वे बहुत रिस्की भी होते हैं। मान लीजिए आपके पास एक हज़ार डॉलर हैं और आप पाँच कंपनियों में दो सौ डॉलर इन्वेस्ट करते हैं। अगर उनमें से कोई एक असाधारण रूप से अच्छा परफॉर्म करती है, जैसे हाल ही में एनवीडिया ने किया। एनवीडिया में आपका दो सौ डॉलर का इन्वेस्टमेंट अकेले छह सौ, आठ सौ या शायद एक हज़ार डॉलर में बदल सकता है, जिससे आपके पोर्टफोलियो गेन्स में काफी बढ़ोतरी होगी। लेकिन इसका दूसरा पहलू ये है, कि अगर आपके पाँच स्टॉक्स में से कोई एक खराब परफॉर्म करता है या दिवालिया हो जाता है, तो आपका नुकसान ओवरऑल मार्केट से ज़्यादा हो सकता है, खासकर बुरे साल में। जैसे, अगर किसी साल मार्केट दस परसेंट नीचे है और आपने ऐसी कंपनियों में इन्वेस्ट किया है जो पचास परसेंट नीचे हैं, तो आप एक वोलेटाइल साल में मार्केट से कम परफॉर्म करेंगे। इंडिविजुअल स्टॉक्स चुनना मार्केट को बीट करने और जल्दी वेल्थ बनाने का एक आकर्षक तरीका हो सकता है। लेकिन अगर आपको रिसर्च पसंद नहीं, या किसी स्टॉक पर ज़्यादा यकीन नहीं, और आप पैसे खोने का रिस्क नहीं लेना चाहते, तो इंडेक्स फंड्स ही आपके लिए बेहतर रहेंगे। इंडेक्स फंड्स एक पैसिव स्ट्रैटेजी है जहाँ आप इसे सेट करके भूल जाते हैं। आप मार्केट या इंडेक्स पर निर्भर रहते हैं, और आपको उनके हिस्टोरिकल एवरेज रिटर्न्स मिलते हैं। S&P 500 में, आपको हर साल आठ से दस परसेंट मिल सकता है। स्टेप नंबर छह: अपने आरएसयू का क्या करना है, अगर आपके पास कोई है तो। आरएसयू का मतलब है रेस्ट्रिक्टेड स्टॉक यूनिट्स, जो एम्प्लॉइज को कंपनी में ज़्यादा समय तक रहने के लिए प्रोत्साहित करते हैं। अगर आप टेस्ला में काम करते हैं और कंपनसेशन पैकेज में चार हज़ार आरएसयू मिलते हैं, तो यह बहुत अच्छा होगा क्योंकि इसकी कीमत एक मिलियन डॉलर से ज़्यादा है। लेकिन आमतौर पर आरएसयू का चार साल का रेस्ट्रिक्टेड वेस्टिंग शेड्यूल होता है, यानी आप एक साल की क्लिफ पर हर साल पच्चीस परसेंट अनलॉक करते हैं। इसका मतलब है कि आपको क्लिफ को पार करने और अपने टेस्ला आरएसयू स्टॉक का पच्चीस परसेंट अनलॉक करने के लिए कम से कम एक साल रहना होगा। फिर उसके बाद, हर महीने आप और ज़्यादा शेयर्स जमा करेंगे और अनलॉक करेंगे। एक साल की क्लिफ इसलिए रखी जाती है ताकि नए एम्प्लॉइज बस अंदर आकर, स्टॉक लेकर, उसे बेचकर, और फिर चले न जाएँ। गाइड कहती है कि आप आरएसयू को वेस्ट होते ही बेच दें, पर मुझे लगता है कि यह आपकी कंपनी और आपको अभी पैसे चाहिए या नहीं, इस पर निर्भर करता है। अगर आप अपनी कंपनी में विश्वास करते हैं और अभी पैसे की ज़रूरत नहीं है, तो शायद आप अपने आरएसयू होल्ड कर सकते हैं, क्योंकि यह वेल्थ बनाने का एक अच्छा तरीका हो सकता है अगर आप उन शेयर्स को होल्ड कर पाते हैं और आपकी कंपनी अच्छा करती है। रेडिट गाइड कहती है कि अगर आपके कुल इन्वेस्टमेंट्स का एक तिहाई से ज़्यादा कंपनी के आरएसयू में लगा है, तो डाइवर्सिफाई करें, जो आमतौर पर बहुत ज़्यादा कंसन्ट्रेटेड होने से बचने के लिए सही है। कुछ मामलों में आप अपनी कंपनी के स्टॉक को होल्ड करना चाहेंगे, खासकर अगर आपकी कंपनी रॉकेट शिप ट्रेजेक्टरी पर है। भले ही आपकी नेट वर्थ अचानक उस कंपनी के स्टॉक का बड़ा हिस्सा बन जाए, फिर भी यह लॉन्ग रन में आपके लिए बेहतर हो सकता है। उदाहरण के लिए, अगर आप एनवीडिया में काम करते हैं और आपके पास आरएसयू थे और इस साल की शुरुआत में, आपको पता था कि एआई बूम ज़ोरों पर था, तो क्या आप सच में उन स्टॉक्स में से एक से डाइवर्सिफाई करना चाहेंगे जिसमें अभी स्टॉक मार्केट में शायद सबसे ज़्यादा मोमेंटम है? शायद नहीं। यदि आपके स्टॉक विकल्प आपकी कंपनी के स्टॉक से खराब हैं, तो विविधता लाने पर विचार करें। आरएसयू को 401k या रॉथ आईआरए जैसे टैक्स-लाभ वाले खातों में स्थानांतरित नहीं किया जा सकता। गाइड का सुझाव है कि उन्हें बेचकर उन पैसों से उन खातों को फंड करें, यदि आपने पहले से नहीं किया है। मैं इससे काफी सहमत हूँ, पर यह आपकी स्थिति पर निर्भर करता है। स्टेप नंबर सात: लॉन्ग-टर्म ग्रोथ के लिए इक्विटीज़ का एक वेल-डाइवर्सिफाइड पोर्टफोलियो रखना। गाइड पचास साल से कम उम्र में अस्सी से सौ परसेंट इक्विटीज़ और कीमती धातुओं या क्रिप्टो जैसे ऑल्टरनेटिव्स में पाँच से दस परसेंट का छोटा एलोकेशन रखने का सुझाव देती है, अगर आप उस रिस्क के साथ कम्फर्टेबल हैं। पचास के बाद, वोलेटिलिटी कम करने और कैपिटल बचाने के लिए सत्तर या शायद साठ परसेंट इक्विटीज़ तक एडजस्ट करना चाहेंगे। यह रेकमेंडेशन काफी सटीक है। मैंने पहले भी शेयर किया है कि आप जितने छोटे होते हैं, उतना ही ज़्यादा रिस्क-ऑन हो सकते हैं, क्योंकि आप वेल्थ बनाने के फेज़ में होते हैं। इस स्टेप के लिए ज़्यादा कमेंट्स नहीं हैं। चलिए स्टेप नंबर आठ पर चलते हैं। स्टेप नंबर आठ: टर्म, डिसेबिलिटी या अम्ब्रेला इंश्योरेंस के ज़रिए इनकम और एसेट्स को प्रोटेक्ट करना। मुझे पर्सनली लगता है कि यह स्टेप ऑप्शनल है, लेकिन अगर आपको इंश्योरेंस चाहिए, तो इस गाइड के अनुसार उनके वेरिएशंस के बारे में बात करते हैं। पहला है टर्म लाइफ इंश्योरेंस। मेरी राय में, यह होल लाइफ से बेहतर है कम प्रीमियम, सीधी शर्तें। गाइड सलाह देती है कि आप अपनी हाउसहोल्ड इनकम का 4-8 गुना लाइफ इंश्योरेंस लें। तो उदाहरण के लिए, छह गुना लेते हैं। मान लीजिए आपकी हाउसहोल्ड इनकम साल की ढाई लाख डॉलर है। तो आपके पास एक लैडर्ड टर्म पॉलिसी हो सकती है जहाँ आप तीस साल के लिए एक मिलियन डॉलर और बीस साल के लिए पाँच लाख के लिए कवर्ड हैं। इस तरह, अगर आप अभी पैंतीस साल के हैं, तो आपके पास पैंसठ साल की उम्र में रिटायरमेंट तक आपको कवर करने वाली एक मिलियन डॉलर की पॉलिसी हो सकती है, और आपके पास एक एक्स्ट्रा पाँच लाख डॉलर होंगे जो आपको पचपन साल की उम्र तक कवर करेंगे, शायद जब आपका मॉर्गेज पूरी तरह से चुका दिया गया हो। तो पैंतीस से पचपन तक, आपके पास कुल डेढ़ मिलियन डॉलर की पॉलिसी होगी, जब आपकी ज़रूरतें सबसे ज़्यादा हों, जैसे बच्चे, मॉर्गेज, वगैरह। और पचपन से पैंसठ तक, आपके पास अभी भी वह एक मिलियन डॉलर का कवरेज होगा, क्योंकि शायद इस समय तक आपके बच्चे कॉलेज चले गए हैं और आपका मॉर्गेज चुका दिया गया है। हालाँकि, पैंसठ साल की उम्र के बाद, आप अपने दम पर हैं। लेकिन टर्म लाइफ इंश्योरेंस आपको मिलने वाले कवरेज के लिए अपेक्षाकृत सस्ता है। तो इस तरह की पॉलिसी के साथ, आपके प्रीमियम महीने में सौ से डेढ़ सौ डॉलर के बीच हो सकते हैं, अह, पंद्रह सौ से दो हज़ार डॉलर प्रति माह की तुलना में जो एक होल लाइफ पॉलिसी हो सकती है। दूसरे प्रकार का इंश्योरेंस है डिसेबिलिटी इंश्योरेंस। अगर आपका प्रोफेशन फिजिकल एबिलिटीज़ पर निर्भर करता है, जैसे आप एक सर्जन हैं, तो ओन ऑक्यूपेशन डिसेबिलिटी पॉलिसी लें। काम नहीं कर सकते तो कमाई प्रोटेक्ट करने के लिए साठ से सत्तर परसेंट इनकम रिप्लेसमेंट का लक्ष्य रखें। ये सब तो इनकम प्रोटेक्शन की बात थी। अब आते हैं तीसरे प्रकार के इंश्योरेंस पर, अम्ब्रेला इंश्योरेंस। ये हाई अर्नर्स के लिए बहुत ही ज़रूरी है, क्योंकि आप जितने ज़्यादा अमीर होते जाते हैं, मुकदमों के लिए उतने ही बड़े टारगेट बन जाते हैं। अम्ब्रेला इंश्योरेंस तब काम आता है जब आपकी रेगुलर ऑटो या होमओनर इंश्योरेंस की लिमिट्स पार हो जाती हैं। मान लीजिए आप किसी गंभीर कार एक्सीडेंट में हैं और कोई आप पर दो मिलियन डॉलर का मुकदमा करता है। आपका ऑटो इंश्योरेंस शायद पाँच लाख डॉलर कवर करेगा, लेकिन इससे आप पर डेढ़ मिलियन डॉलर की ज़िम्मेदारी आ जाएगी। यह आपकी पूरी नेट वर्थ और भविष्य की कमाई को खत्म कर सकता है, लेकिन एक दो मिलियन डॉलर की अम्ब्रेला पॉलिसी उस गैप को भर देगी। गाइड कम से कम एक मिलियन डॉलर के अम्ब्रेला कवरेज की सलाह देती है, लेकिन एक हाई अर्नर के रूप में, आप शायद दो मिलियन डॉलर से ज़्यादा कंसीडर करना चाहेंगे। इस इंश्योरेंस की कॉस्ट काफी सस्ती है। यह अक्सर एक मिलियन डॉलर के कवरेज के लिए सिर्फ दो सौ से चार सौ डॉलर प्रति वर्ष होता है, और हर एडिशनल मिलियन पर शायद सिर्फ सौ या दो सौ डॉलर प्रति वर्ष और लगेंगे। यह निश्चित रूप से रखना अच्छा है। अगर आप हाई अर्नर हैं, तो आपको इनमें से किसी इंश्योरेंस की ज़रूरत नहीं है, लेकिन कुछ लोगों को इन्हें रखना पसंद होता है। यह हाई इनकम अर्नर्स के लिए एक कंप्लीट आठ-स्टेप फाइनेंशियल प्लेबुक थी, जिसे महत्व के क्रम में प्राथमिकता दी गई है। इस फ्रेमवर्क में मुझे पसंद है कि यह अंदाज़ा मिटा देता है कि अगला डॉलर कहाँ जाए, और उस रेडिट उपयोगकर्ता को धन्यवाद जिसने इसे संकलित किया। एक हाई अर्नर होना सिर्फ बहुत सारा पैसा कमाना नहीं है, बल्कि यह उस पैसे को सही तरीके से मैनेज करके, उसे लंबे समय तक चलने वाली और टिकाऊ वेल्थ में बदलने के बारे में है। आप अभी किस स्टेप पर काम कर रहे हैं? नीचे कमेंट्स में बताएं। अगर आप मेरे इस वीडियो में इंटरेस्टेड हैं कि अमीर लोग पैसे के बारे में कैसे सोचना पसंद करते हैं, तो आप यहाँ यह वीडियो देख सकते हैं। यह टॉप एक परसेंट और उससे आगे के लोगों की मानसिकता पर एक अच्छा वीडियो है। मैं आप लोगों से उस वीडियो में या इस चैनल पर भविष्य के किसी वीडियो   1. @johnbuehler3 [1 likes]: What is your recommendation for someone that wants to retire by the age of 50 and is considering investing in a brokerage account to bridge the 10 yr gap before they’re able to withdrawal penalty free from their IRA and 401k. Although they’re contributing to their IRA and 401k, they’re not maxing them out.
  2. @MarkInvestsUSA: Love the focus on practical advice!
  3. @johnmichaelmorrow1589: This is a great guide for above average income but, for people with truly high income this guide is a ticking time bomb for taxes. Most people will save for retirement then retire and spend down their accounts into their 80s and then die and leave their home to heirs. This advice is adequate for that person; however, for truly high income they will never spend all their money. They might spend 25k/m or 300k/yr while netting over 500k+ in income. In that range they will likely hit retirement with well over 10M in assets. When they retire they can replace their income using only 3% or less of their portfolio meaning they will probably die leaving over 40M to their heirs. If you own real estate and individual stocks their is a step up in basis and the heirs pay no income or capital gains taxes to receive those dollars, but if they have 10M plus in retirement accounts those could be double taxed meaning estate taxes and income taxes on those accounts. This could leave over 2/3rds of retirement accounts to the IRS at death not to mention RMDs which force that retiree into the highest tax bracket during retirement. You might say to avoid that just spend down retirement accounts first before brokerage accounts to avoid the tax trap at death; however, selling individual stocks is a 23.8% maximum CG tax and liquidating retirement accounts is a 37% maximum federal income tax (not including state taxes) no one will willing pay 37% over 23.8% meaning the retirement accounts never get touched and largely go the IRS at death. Once you reach the point that you are able to save over about 100k/yr you take the employee match and that’s it. no more dollars to tax deferred accounts. Hope this helps anymore making over about 400k-500k/yr.

  1. @libragirl7171 [1 likes]: Always a good time with Luke Carl and friends!!
  2. @mdindestin: Great show.  My wife and I are crushing it, but there is always room for improvement.  Interesting perspectives.  

I would say the biggest problem for owners is linens.  New owners buy super high thread count sheets, luxury towels, etc.  Then, they're aghast when the cleaner just takes them with her to wash them and introduces them into the rotation with all the rest of their client's units.  

The owners are so mad they could spit on the ground and an adversarial relationship starts from the very beginning and they think short term rentals suck.  

It helps to understand the cleaner's perspective on this issue, don't judge them and don't take it personal.  Have the conversation with your cleaner up front, but they don't because new owners don't know what they don't know.
  3. @denissewullschleger7803: turno  is the worst! It takes a huge percentage of each cleaning and also takes days to pay to the cleaner / none cleaner is happy working through turno ! Absolutely no!

to get up and clean and you will certainly procrastinate it for tomorrow leaving you a stressful and messy morning so resist that urge to crash rather force clean up then relax tip number nine donate to clean and organize keep stuff that you will really use and sparks joy in you as mary kondo says donate the stuff that do not spark joy there is more joy in owning less than can be found in owning more and it is far better to donate than accumulate tip number 10 keeping floors clean we all know how quickly dirt dust and grime builds up prevention is better than cure use doormats at entrance take your shoes off and ask guests to do likewise too immediately wipe up any spills especially on porous surfaces like marble other than that sadly you've simply got to clean girl so invest in a good vacuum that will suck all the dirt and dust off the floor rather than sweeping or brooming that kind of stirred from one place to the another also having a spinner mop is great as its microfiber mop head is idle for all flows and no bending required though our indian way of mopping is great workout it takes much longer to mop compared to a spin mop mopping the flow every day is a good practice as it adds a lot of positivity and freshness to the room instead of adding disinfectant to the water simply add some sea salt and then do the mopping this will take away much of the negative energy and will raise the vibrations of the room salt is very effective way to get rid of pollution inside your home add one spoon salt to a bucket of water at least twice a week salt has been used since ages by our grandmothers to remove evil eye by others so give it a try tip number eleven clean up spillages immediately this is no brainer clean up spillages immediately rather than getting it dry as dry ventilate you can even squeegee to take off excess water of glass and the walls vinegar will prevent any further mold formation however it will not magically remove the mould that is already sitting - The Secret to GREAT Customer Service | Simon Sinek: Kind: captions Language: en Customers talk about they have great service or bad service if they got what they want or didn't get what they want. Right? But that's not true. I'll give you an example. Let's say I'm on a business trip and my work is done early and I want to go home to my family. I check the website. I see that there are there's flights available on the earlier flights. Let's call it a day early. Um and so I call the airline and I say, "Hey, I'm done early. I'd really love to get home to my family. I see that you've got availability in the earlier flight. Can I get on that flight? And the agent says, "I'm sorry, you have the wrong class of ticket. I can't change your I can't change your flight. I'm happy to pay a fee. I'm happy to pay whatever it is, please. Can I get on that flight? I'm sorry, sir. I told you you have the wrong class of uh you have the wrong class. I can't I can't book you. You have the wrong status flight. There's nothing I can do. The computer won't let me do it." Okay. I now hate that airline, right? Okay. Let's replay that. Okay. Same scenario. Hi, I'm done early. Love to go home. I see you have availability. Can I get on that flight? Sir, I want to get you home to your family. Here's the problem. Your your your ticket is the wrong class and so the computer won't let me. But let me let me see what I can do. I really want to get you home to your family. Click click click click click click click. That didn't work. Click click click click. That didn't work. Sir, let me talk to my supervisor. I'm determined to get you home to your family. Go away. Come back. Sir, I talked to my supervisor. We've tried like six different things. There's nothing I can do. I am so so sorry. I just cannot get you on that earlier flight. Don't worry. Thank you for trying. I now love that airline even though I didn't get what I want. And that's what it feels like when someone cares. It doesn't mean we always get what we want, but what we get is somebody who understands and cares about what we're trying to accomplish. That's considered good service.

- Customer Service Vs. For instance, list five companies and to the right say, "I go to this restaurant at least once a week. Why do I go back? Because of . . . " I go to this fast food place every week. Why. . . I go to this store to shop for clothes every month. Why do I go back again? I go here for groceries. Why do I go back here over and over again? What is the reason? And the reason will generally be one of these six reasons that it's going to give you a lot of clues for your own business. #1: Fast You're either going back there because it's fast. Why? Fast food - I don't want to sit down. I just want my food and leave. That's all I want. I don't want anything. I just want a fast service. So you go back to a place because it's fast. #2: Quality One could be quality. What is quality? It's better material. It's better fabric. It's better whatever. The quality is better and I'm willing to spend more money but I go back for quality. #3: Cheap Another reason could be because it's cheap. It's written about in books, etc. etc. This person goes to Nordstrom and says, "I bought this tire from here." The cashier says, "We don't sell tires." "No, I bought it here, and I want to return it." "Do you have a receipt?" "I don't have a receipt." Do you know what Nordstrom did? They took the tire! And the person said how much it cost and Nordstrom said, "Okay, we'll take it back." And the person went on to say we returned the tire, they're going to throw that tire in the trash, but customer service to them is extremely important. Now there are some places you buy something from, no return policy. We don't take it back. We don't. . . they don't care having one star reviews because their policy is, I don't care to return it. You buy it, you got it, it's gone, we're not taking it back. Mattress companies sometimes do that. Some of them do certain - no, you've already laid on it. You've already done this. We're not taking any of it back. If you think about Amazon, Amazon's got great customer service. I think about Zappos. We went and got a private tour of Zappos headquarters. They'll stay on the phone with you and talk to you. They literally will talk to you, how things are, hey let me order pizza for you. Hey, let me do this for you. It's constant customer service that people talk about them. Apple's known for great customer service. Southwest Airlines, when their attendants tell the jokes that they say, "hey, if you have to do this, make sure you put the mask on yourself and if you have two kids, choose which one you like more. . ." These things hahaha, it's funny; but that's customer service. You kind of connect with it. This is a very, very, very valuable way for you to build your business. So now the question becomes, what is the difference between customer service and customer experience. Pat, what is the difference? Isn't it the same thing? Absolutely not. Let me explain. And I'll simplify it for you and it will stay in your mind. Think about customer service as reactive. And think about customer experience as proactive. What does this mean? For instance. Here's customer service. "Thank you for calling AT&T. How may I help you?" Yes, great. Is there any other issues that you have? Have I fulfilled your service request you've asked here today? Okay, please take this recorded call and survey that we have going on . . . Reactive. Customer service could be a client is upset, you handle it, you react, you solve. All good. Customer service is thank you, please, may I please put you on hold? Thank you for your help. Thank you for listening. Thank you for understanding. Thank you. . . it's customer service. Customer service could be, "I totally understand where you're coming from. I totally feel your pain. I'm sorry." It's apologetic. It's customer service, because it's reactive to something, right? A customer buys, here's your receipt, thank you so much, have a great day, would you also like to buy this? Would you like to upgrade your service? That's customer service. And by the way, very important, customer service. Now a few are very good at customer experience. That's kind of when you go from here [customer service] and you graduate over here [customer experience]. Let me explain what I mean by customer experience. Look, whatever business you run, if you have more than 100 customers, you will generally be able to tell me that you have some VIP customers. Everybody has VIP customers. This is not about discrimination. VIP customers to American Express is called Black Card. VIP customers to American Airlines is called Executive Platinum or Executive or Gold Member, that they get priority seating, right? A VIP customer for Sephora makeup for ladies could be called Beauty Insider. Sephora does very well because they have this Beauty Insider group where the girls who are part of Beauty Insider get samples sent to them before everybody else. That's Sephora. They do a phenomenal job with Beauty Insider that's worked very, very well. They get it before all the other customers. Your VIP, VIP Amazon is Prime. There are many of them that I can give you. The VIPs, you're hoping to get many of them here [customer service] to be converted in to VIPs and they only get converted here, based on what? Customer experience. So what is customer experience? Proactive. Proactive is the way you treat them. The details. Customer experience is all about details. So if I call American Express Black Card, or if I call Visa Black Card, Mastercard Black Card and they'll tell me, "Hey, we just want to remind you, your anniversary is coming up. Your wife's birthday is coming up. Any plans? We see that last time you went to Bacara Hotel. Do you want to do the same thing? Do you want me to look for something? How about you give me this afternoon and let me look for something and get back to you." Then they send me an email and they call me. When is a good time for us to get on the phone? 15 minutes. So let me tell you what I found. If you want to do something very extravagant, we found this one place and we can do this experience and . . . and it's do unique and let me tell you what it is. You're wife's really going to like it. Tell me what things your wife likes. Experience. Amazon could go out there and find exactly what things you buy and see your buying trends and they give you a better experience. It doesn't matter what it is. it all goes to the experience part. Or you go into Apple and you talk to one of the guys at Apple and they talk to you like you are a regular human being. You don't feel like you're scripted -- too much. Although they have a script as well that they've got to follow. But it's just talking to you. Zappos the same way. They're talking to you. They're listening to you. Tell me about. . . how's your day? You don't hear too many "regular" customer service people companies saying, "So tell me about your day." Think about that small little side question that you call somewhere, you call Chase for Yes, I'm trying to handle this. . . Okay, great, let me place you on hold. Let me see what I can do. Do you have a case number for this? Okay, thank you. Okay, have a great day. Bye bye. Done. Instead, okay, Patrick Bet-David, thank you so much. By the way, Patrick, how was your day today?" And I'm going to say whatever I'm going to say. I'm going to give them, "It's okay. It's great" And then I'm going to ask what? "How was your day?" "Oh, my day's great. Thank you for asking. This [customer service] goes to here [customer And the more you can take people from here [customer service] to here [customer experience] the better off you're going to be with your business. Let me help you think about it maybe in a way that will make sense to you a little bit more and we'll wrap up this episode is this. So when it comes down to the dating game. Just think about it this way. There are a lot of people that just [snap] boom. They want to go out and be promiscuous. It's a philosophy, if you want to do that, that's good. In business, if you treat your customers like that or even women like that, your reputation spreads. If you treat customers like that, or anybody like that, reputation spreads. And it's not always the best reputation to not treat people properly. So then there is just one-night stand philosophy. Then there's dating philosophy. Then there's marriage philosophy. Your VIPs are marriage. Your VIPs are people that go from dating to marriage. They're kind of trying your product. They like it. Ah, um, great. Wow, they treat me so well, I want to marry these guys. And I want to go there regularly. And then I want to tell other people. They become your best recruiters. VIP members become your best recruiters. Best recruiters. They recruit for you for free. All that million dollar ad on T.V. that you're trying to do or whatever it is you're trying to do, you don't have to do it. Because they're doing it for free - if you convert people into VIPs. They are so valuable. This is why some companies, there's nothing different Zappos does except for the fact that they create incredible customer service. They don't make shoes. Zappos doesn't make shoes. They're not Toms. It's service. It's purely service for them. They give incredible service. They send you return, you don't have to pay for return if you don't like it, they'll pay for it. It's purely a service game. They're not playing the cheap game. They're playing the service game with you. It's so valuable and then all of a sudden, Zappos gets valued at, it was bought for a couple billion dollars, whatever the number Tony Hsieh is a customer service guy. So I know a lot of times especially startup entrepreneurs are people that you want to become a millionaire yesterday. I get messages, "I want to be a billionaire by the time I'm 35 years old." That's great, you know. Maybe don't tie it to, believe me, if you tie it for more people being happy doing business - I had a conversation this morning with a friend of mine who's one of our executives in our company, he's a doctor, Dr. Cooper. Now we've become very good friends, so it's past a VIP. And the more we have of that, that we treat people properly, the more they tell everybody. Your business gets stronger.
